for lower-grid-access locations, deployed across public
spaces and partner institutions. My engineering job was
to make the software side of that combined operation
legible and useful: the live Google Maps dispenser
directory that lets users locate the nearest installation;
the bilingual advocacy document distribution flow; the
live product-demo interface; and the locations dataset
that tracks each rollout. This was an explicit exercise
in shipping software that is judged not on technical
novelty but on whether it makes physical-world deployments
visible, addressable, and trustworthy to non-technical
audiences.
### Backend Developer — Youth Training Camp (青训营) at ByteDance
[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-18]
- **Period:** Nov 2024 -- Mar 2025
- **Location:** China (remote)
- **URL:** https://youthcamp.bytedance.com/

**Impact:** #2-most-prolific committer on the team's Spring Boot capstone (26% of 174 commits) and published two open-source backend-architecture templates (Go + Java with bilingual READMEs) — now used by later ByteDance Youth Training Camp cohorts as scaffolding.

**Company context:** ByteDance — the parent company of Douyin (Chinese TikTok), TikTok,
Toutiao, CapCut, Lark, and Doubao — runs its **Youth Training
Camp (青训营)** as a structured cohort programme for backend,
frontend, mobile, and algorithm-engineering talent in China.
The camp combines an algorithm curriculum delivered on
ByteDance's MarsCode platform (hosted at juejin.cn) with a
capstone team project where developers ship a complete
production-shape system end-to-end. It is one of the most
competitive training entry points into China's domestic
consumer-internet stack, and the project teams are explicitly
modelled on the engineering practices of ByteDance product
teams — clean architecture, mainstream stacks (Go / Java /
Vue), bilingual documentation, and visible code review.

**Mission:** Move trainees from "can write working code" to "can ship
production-grade systems in a team" by simulating ByteDance's
own engineering rigour — disciplined algorithm fluency,
clean-architecture backend design, real-world e-commerce
domain modelling, and the kind of git-history hygiene and
bilingual-documentation expectation that matters for working
inside a Chinese megacorp with global reach.

**My mandate:** Joined the **"Rebirth: I Code at Youth Training Camp" cohort
(重生之我在青训营写代码), Team 115**, as backend developer on
the camp's capstone e-commerce platform — *Douyin Mall*. The
mandate had three pieces: (1) complete ByteDance MarsCode's
algorithm curriculum at depth — not just pass, but solve and
write up each problem; (2) ship backend features on the
Team-115 capstone alongside seven other trainees, with the
normal expectations of a cross-functional engineering team
(branching, code review, integration testing, deployment);
(3) carry the work outwards beyond the camp's required scope
if I had the energy — which I did.

**Impact delivered:** **Algorithm curriculum — 81 problems, fully written up.**
Solved every problem in ByteDance's MarsCode Youth Training
Camp Juejin algorithm track and published them as a public
teaching repository at *juejin-algorithm-practice* (66
commits, 100% solo). Each problem ships with a per-problem
README (problem statement, examples, solution approach,
complexity analysis) and a tested Python implementation.
Topics span arrays/strings, dynamic programming, greedy
algorithms, math and number theory, binary search, graphs and
trees, bit manipulation, sorting and searching, simulation,
and encoding/decoding — the breadth a Chinese backend
engineer is expected to demonstrate at interview.

**Team 115 capstone — Douyin Mall backend.** Shipped backend
features on the team's Spring Boot 3 e-commerce platform
covering user management, product management, shopping cart,
order processing, and an AI customer-service module. The
repo carries 174 commits from 8 contributors; **my 45
commits placed me as the #2-most-prolific committer on the
team** (~26% of the codebase's commit history), behind only
the team lead. Stack: Java 17 + Spring Boot 3 + MyBatis +
MySQL + Redis + Alibaba Cloud services + Docker, with the
kind of microservice-style internal layering the camp's
evaluation rubric expects.

**Frontend supplement — built solo to integrate against the
team backend.** Authored a separate Vue 3 + Vite + TypeScript
+ Pinia + Vue Router frontend (`douyin-mall-frontend`, 15
commits, 100% mine) with end-to-end Cypress and Vitest unit
coverage, so the team's backend had a clean live-integration
target. This wasn't required by the camp; I wrote it to close
the loop on the system as a working product.

**Two open-source teaching templates distilled from the
capstone.** The camp's full-stack architecture had enough
reusable shape to be worth packaging as starter kits for the
next cohort of learners, so I built and published two
clean-architecture skeletons:

- **`douyin-mall-go-template`** (a.k.a. "TikTok Shop Go
  Template") — Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis backend with
  standard `cmd/`, `internal/`, `api/`, `pkg/`, `configs/`,
  `docs/`, `frontend/`, `logs/` layout, JWT auth, RESTful
  APIs, and a paired React frontend — 27 commits, 100%
  solo, bilingual EN + 简体中文 README, 53 GitHub stars —
  the strongest external traction of any of the camp's
  artefacts, though it now sits in the archived band of
  Chan's open-source catalogue rather than the active
  README lineup.
- **`douyin-mall-java-template`** — the Spring Boot + Maven
  counterpart with Maven wrapper, conventional `src/main` /
  `src/test` layout — 13 commits, 100% solo, bilingual
  README. Intended as the Java track's mirror of the Go
  template.

The five repositories together — algorithm track, capstone
backend, supplementary frontend, two architecture templates
— represent a deliberately overcomplete portfolio of camp
artefacts: most trainees deliver the algorithm submissions
and the team capstone; very few add a solo frontend and two
production-shape architecture templates on top.

**Technical highlights:** **Java / Spring Boot 3 + MyBatis + MySQL + Redis +
Alibaba Cloud** on the Team-115 capstone, which is the
canonical Chinese-internet backend stack. The architecture
is layered along the lines mainstream ByteDance projects
use — controller / service / mapper separation, Redis-
backed caching for hot product and cart paths, MyBatis for
explicit SQL control rather than JPA's abstraction, and
Alibaba Cloud services for OSS / SMS / message queue
integration. This is the stack a Chinese backend engineer
is expected to have shipped in production.

**Go + Gin clean-architecture template** in
`douyin-mall-go-template`. The directory shape (`cmd/`,
`internal/`, `pkg/`, `api/`, `configs/`, `docs/`, `logs/`,
`frontend/`) is the layout the Go community treats as
production-correct — separation between executable entry
points, internal packages, and reusable libraries; explicit
config and docs trees; logs as a deployment artefact. The
template ships JWT authentication, RESTful API conventions,
and a complete e-commerce domain model so that a learner
forking it has the shape of a real service rather than a
scaffolded stub.

**Vue 3 + Vite + Pinia + Vue Router + TypeScript** on the
frontend supplement, with a non-trivial testing pyramid
(Vitest for unit, Cypress for e2e via `start-server-and-
test`) — explicitly the configuration that lets you run
e2e tests both in CI (`vite preview` + `cypress run`) and
interactively (`vite dev` + `cypress open`).

**Algorithm fluency across ten topic areas** — the 81
problems on Juejin span the topic distribution Chinese
big-tech interview panels actually test, including the
weaker spots most candidates have (bit manipulation,
simulation, encoding/decoding) rather than just the Leetcode
favourites (arrays, DP). Each solution has explicit
complexity analysis, which is how the camp graded them.

**Bilingual technical documentation discipline** — every
repository I authored or led for the camp ships
side-by-side English and 简体中文 READMEs at production
quality. This is the documentation expectation inside a
Chinese megacorp with global products, and it's a habit I
carried out of the camp into every subsequent project.

**Team collaboration at the right scale** — being the #2
committer on an 8-person team taught the specific muscles
that solo projects don't: PR review etiquette, integration
conflict resolution, sharing test fixtures across modules,
and writing commit messages that another contributor will
read.
### Full Stack Developer & Lead Documenter (Lincoln University COMP693 Industry Placement) at CORDE (NZ infrastructure & civil construction)
[tier=secondary · recency=historical · updated=2026-05-18]
- **Period:** Jun 2024 -- Nov 2024
- **Location:** Canterbury, New Zealand
- **URL:** https://corde.nz/

**Impact:** #1-most-prolific contributor (42% of 539 commits) on CORDE's offline-first React Native field-operations app — still widely used today by CORDE's water/civil-infrastructure crews. Also owned 97.9% of the documentation track end-to-end.

**Company context:** **CORDE** is a Canterbury-based **New Zealand infrastructure
and civil-construction company** — 30+ years of operations,
roughly 164 staff (LinkedIn category 201–500), trading
nationally under the tagline *"Trusted Connections."* In
February 2022 three separate Canterbury entities were unified
under the CORDE brand. The company operates across three
specialist arms — **Civil Construction**, **Regional Asset
Maintenance** (open-space management; introduced autonomous
mowing tech to NZ via partnerships with Colbrook Industries
and RC Mowers USA), and **Water Infrastructure / three waters**
— across three Canterbury offices: head office at **85
Hoskyns Road, Rolleston**, plus **Timaru** (South Canterbury)
and **Rangiora** (North Canterbury). Recognised projects
include Te Whāriki Subdivision Stage 3, Windsorcare Village
Stage 1, and the Ellesmere-to-Pines Pipeline Project; awards
include **CCNZ Canterbury Contractor of the Year** and a
**Construction Excellence National Award**. CORDE recently
acquired Arnold Jensen Electrical (70+-year-old NZ electrical
specialist) to bring electrical capability in-house. This
is a substantial regional employer, not a small client.

The engagement was a **Lincoln University COMP693 industry-
placement capstone** — the practicum module of the Master of
Applied Computing programme — embedded directly inside CORDE
with weekly on-site meetings at their Canterbury offices,
distributed online work between meetings, and CORDE's own
engineering processes (Azure DevOps for project management,
Figma for design hand-off, Apifox + Swagger for API contract
testing) governing how the work shipped. Five months from
June 2024 kickoff to November 2024 delivery.

**Mission:** Solve a concrete operational problem inside a working
infrastructure firm: CORDE's field crews — water-
infrastructure technicians, civil-construction site teams,
regional-asset-maintenance operators — routinely work in
rural and semi-rural Canterbury where mobile network coverage
is unreliable. The company's central operations platform
(referred to as "Workbench" in the API contracts) is web-
based and assumes connectivity. The mobile app's job was to
make the field worker's tablet the **system of record for
the moment of work**, capture text and photos and GPS
simultaneously, hold it locally with integrity guarantees,
and synchronise back to Workbench cleanly when connectivity
returned — including handling the conflict cases that real
offline-first systems have to handle. This is a real
productivity problem worth real engineering for a 164-staff
infrastructure operator.

**My mandate:** Full-stack developer on a two-active-developer Lincoln
University team (myself plus Luke Shi / Zhenzhou Shi),
reporting directly to **Barry Clark** on CORDE's side as
the business sponsor and to **Kullum Ladley** as CORDE's
technical liaison and Workbench API specification author.
Mandate covered: front-end implementation across the entire
React Native + Native Base UI layer, database design (ERD
work co-designed with CORDE's existing database slices),
business-logic diagrams and architectural documentation,
weekly sprint-meeting question sets sent to CORDE, stage-
development presentation decks, live app demos and screen
recordings, and APK packaging for weekly CORDE reviews.
Documentation lead was an explicit role inside the team —
the docs repository carries a top-level folder literally
titled "*Chan Meng's Documentation Contribution Collection*"
with separate sub-folders for each of those mandates.

**Impact delivered:** **Shipped a production-quality React Native offline-data-
collection mobile app** for CORDE's field-operations
workflow. The codebase carries **539 commits across three
contributors**; **my 227 commits (combining the Chan Meng
and Chan handles) make me the #1-most-prolific contributor
— 42% of the entire commit history** — with Luke Shi (the
repo owner under his GitHub handle gentoo111) on 28.6% and
Xue Zheng on 25.8%. The app integrates with CORDE's
Workbench central operations platform via the API contract
documented across a sequence of **15 specifications
authored by Kullum Ladley** (the same Kullum named in every
API document's filename prefix `YYYYMMDD_Kullum_...`),
covering Workbench API calls, log creation, mobile-app
authentication, log extension fields, file-upload pipelines,
and group completion workflow — all captured under version
control as the API/ folder of the team's documentation
repository.

**Owned the documentation track end-to-end.** The team's
documentation repository (`team-corde`) has 143 commits;
**my 140 (97.9%) make me effectively the sole author.**
The repo contains: day-by-day weekly project work records
(the visible diary of the engagement across five months —
every UI library evaluation, every database-schema
iteration, every employer-email outcome, every offline
meeting decision), the project description and user-stories
archive, the full API documentation set from CORDE, and
seven separate sub-folders enumerating my specific
contributions — business logic diagrams, presentation
decks, database design, UI/UX and front-end implementation,
live demos and screen recordings, APK packaging, and
weekly sprint question sets.

**Practised the soft skills the capstone is designed to
teach.** Translated CORDE's abstract user stories into
concrete Azure DevOps work items; co-designed the database
schema by reconciling two team-developed plans against
CORDE's existing database slices into a unified version;
survived two UI library re-evaluations (Native Base →
React Native Elements → Native Base) and chose Native Base
for its Figma-component-library integration so design and
implementation stayed close; ran the demo cadence in front
of Barry Clark and the CORDE technical team every week
across five months; and made the team's GitHub one-branch
policy work even though it required disciplined sequencing
of two developers' parallel work.

**Kept the codebase modern after the engagement ended.**
Continued making targeted commits in subsequent years for
React Native version currency, Android 16 KB page-size
linker compliance, husky + lint-staged pre-commit hooks,
and dependency upgrades — keeping the repository a usable
portfolio piece rather than an archived snapshot of
mid-2024 React Native conventions.

**The application remains in active use today by CORDE's
field crews** for offline data collection — GPS-tagged
photos and structured form data captured on water-
infrastructure and civil-construction sites where mobile
coverage drops out, then syncing back when crews return
to coverage. The codebase-currency commits aren't archive
maintenance; they're keeping a production tool aligned
with the platform conventions of the year the crews are
actually running it on.

**Technical highlights:** **React Native 0.83 + TypeScript + Native Base** on the
client, deliberately chosen over Expo because the project's
offline-first requirements pushed against Expo's managed
workflow (and over React Native Elements because Native
Base shipped a Figma component library that let the team
do design-first then implement against the exact
components). The native-only mode also opened the door to
Android-native development where the offline pipeline
needed it.

**SQLite as the system of record for the moment of work.**
Local-first storage of text + photo + GPS coordinates +
timestamps captured in the field, with a synchronisation
pipeline that reconciles back to CORDE's Workbench central
database when connectivity returns. The sync model had to
handle the real conflict cases offline-first systems
actually face — duplicate-key collisions when a worker's
local IDs and the server's IDs both move forward, partial
sync where one log uploads but its attachments don't,
connectivity drop mid-batch, and the file-upload
sub-protocol covered separately in Kullum's API specifications.

**Heavy native-integration surface:** geolocation +
geolocation-service (foreground and background), camera-
roll, image-picker, document-picker, file-system, secure
keychain storage, native maps, react-native-permissions
for the Android permission flow, base64 encoding for the
on-the-wire payloads. The dependency list is twice as long
as a typical RN app because field-data-collection in
Canterbury's rural water-infrastructure context genuinely
touches that much of the device.

**API integration discipline.** Apifox for shared interface
testing across the team; Swagger as the contract source
from CORDE; a `test-sharepoint-connection.js` script as
evidence of a SharePoint-touching corner of CORDE's
document workflow; Azure DevOps as the work-item store
Barry Clark and the CORDE team worked from; per-week
question sets compiled into documents and reviewed before
each offline meeting so CORDE's time was used efficiently.

**The documentation repo is the artefact.** A second
repository (`team-corde`) ran in parallel as the project's
living memory — every weekly meeting decision, every
UI-library evaluation, every database-schema iteration,
every CORDE email exchange's outcome, captured in
version-controlled prose. This is unusually disciplined
documentation for a student-team engagement and is the
practice I carried into every subsequent role.

## Volunteer Experience

### STEM Mentor at Forward with Her (她行)
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-06-04]
- **Period:** Oct 2024 -- Oct 2025
- **Location:** Remote (cross-Pacific)
- **URL:** https://www.linkedin.com/company/taxing-mentorship/
- **Keywords:** STEM mentorship, Women in tech, Career development, Bi-weekly 1:1 mentorship, Cross-Pacific volunteer network

Volunteer mentor at **Forward with Her (她行)**, a nonprofit founded in
2020 connecting women in STEM through one-on-one and group mentorship.
The organisation has **over 1,000 members** worldwide and launched the
"Her Voice" podcast to amplify women's voices in tech. Bi-weekly
mentorship meetings with assigned early-career mentees cover career
development, personal growth, and practical problem-solving in STEM
environments — coordinated through a dedicated volunteer liaison.
Holds 2024 and 2025 official mentor certificates.

bridging the gender gap in STEM (where female representation sits at
~20%). She Sharp has built a **2,200+ member community** and organised
**84+ events since 2014**. Continues Chan's relationship with the
organisation after the formal Lead Engineer role, focused on
sustaining the digital infrastructure that powers community growth.
### Volunteer Educator at Cebu International Academy (CIA)

and Business English programmes with focused small-group training).
Actively led community outreach programmes in rural Cebu —
organising events and distributing essential supplies to children,
women, and the elderly. The role strengthened business communication
skills and reinforced a commitment to driving positive social impact
through technology.
### Festival Translator & Cultural Exchange Volunteer at Art Committee of Guilin Festival
[tier=archive · recency=historical · updated=2026-05-18]
- **Period:** Sep 2023 -- Nov 2023
- **Location:** Guilin, Guangxi, China
- **Keywords:** Cross-cultural exchange, Māori cultural studies, Translation, Cultural representation, Business English

Volunteer translator for a **Māori dance troupe from New Zealand**
at the **2023 Guilin Arts Festival**. Provided translation support
and facilitated deeper cultural exchange between performers and
local audiences — covering traditional dance, music, and language.
The placement sharpened cross-cultural communication skills and
illustrated how technology can enrich cultural representation in
international settings. An early signal of the cross-Pacific
orientation that later anchored Chan's work between China and NZ.

## Flagship Projects

### 1. Google News MCP Server — Early-ecosystem MCP server (shipped 5 weeks after Anthropic's Nov 25 2024 MCP launch) — PulseMCP "Top Pick", featured in Skywork AI deep-dive review, distributed across 15+ MCP catalogs
[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/server-google-news
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/server-google-news
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript, Node.js, @modelcontextprotocol/sdk, stdio transport, SerpAPI, axios, ES modules, npm (scoped @chanmeng666)
- **First commit:** 2024-12-30 — 35 days after MCP's Nov 25 2024 public launch
- **npm package:** @chanmeng666/google-news-server
- **Registry listings:** 15+ MCP catalogs (Smithery, mcp.so, Glama, PulseMCP, Awesome MCP Servers, MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive, Playbooks, mcpservers.org, Archestra, serp.ai, mcp-archive.com, AIBase, AgentSkillsHub, MagicSlides)
- **PulseMCP badge:** Top Pick · 26.1k visitors · #1,105 global rank
- **Glama quality:** A (license) · 122 stars
- **Skywork review:** Dedicated Oct 2025 deep-dive article + AI-Engineer's Guide companion piece
- **Stars:** ~125 GitHub · 20 forks · listed on Smithery, Glama, PulseMCP (Top Pick), Archestra, mcp.so, Enterprise DNA, Augment Code, serp.ai
- **Forks:** 20
- **Commits:** not tracked (shallow history) — see repos
- **Early MCP contrib:** Issue #76 (Nov 27 2024 — Windows/NVM workaround)
**Summary:** An early Model Context Protocol server — the open standard Anthropic
launched in November 2024 to connect AI assistants to outside tools.
Its first commit landed 35 days after that launch. It lets assistants
like Claude, Cursor, and Cline search live Google News through SerpAPI,
automatically sorted into topics (technology, business, science,
health) and offered in 10 languages. Published on npm as
`@chanmeng666/google-news-server`, it carries a PulseMCP "Top Pick"
badge and ships a one-click install bundle for Claude Desktop.

**Impact:** Lets AI assistants like Claude search live Google News — sorted into topics, in 10 languages. One of the first tools built for Anthropic's Model Context Protocol: shipped 35 days after the standard launched, and it's now listed across 15+ MCP directories.

**Problem:** Anthropic published the Model Context Protocol in November 2024 — the
open standard that lets AI assistants like Claude reach outside their
training data to real tools and live sources. In the first weeks after
launch the ecosystem had only a handful of reference servers, and none
of them let an assistant search current news. That was a concrete gap:
people were already asking their AI assistant "what's happening with X
today," a question no model can answer from training data alone, and
there was no Google News option in the MCP catalog.

A second friction appeared at the same time: Windows users running Node
Version Manager couldn't get *any* npm-based MCP server to connect to
Claude Desktop, because PATH resolution through NVM's shim layer broke
the desktop client's ability to launch the server process. That blocked
a large share of early Windows adopters from using MCP at all.

**Audience:** AI assistants and the developers wiring them up. Direct consumers:
Claude Desktop, Cursor's agent mode, Cline, Continue, and any
MCP-compliant client whose setup includes news search. Downstream: the
people asking those assistants about current events — analysts and
journalists monitoring coverage, developers building research and
content agents. The project is deliberately listed on multiple MCP
registries because that is how AI developers actually find servers —
they browse catalogs, not GitHub search.

**Product logic:** A user asks their AI assistant something like "what's happening with
[topic] in [region] this week." The assistant calls the server's one
tool, `google_news_search`, with a query plus optional `gl` (country)
and `hl` (language) codes. The server passes the request to SerpAPI's
Google News engine, gets structured results back, sorts them into
topics (AI & Technology, Business, Science & Research, Healthcare,
General) with lightweight pattern matching, and returns clean output
with each article's source, date, snippet, and link intact. It also
supports search by news topic, publisher, full-story coverage, and
section via token parameters, and falls back to English for
unsupported language codes.

The server runs over MCP's stdio channel — the default for desktop AI
clients — and installs in one command through Smithery's CLI (`npx -y
@smithery/cli install @chanmeng666/google-news-server --client
claude`), or via a packaged `.mcpb` bundle for one-click setup in
Claude Desktop.

**Business logic:** Open source, MIT licensed, no direct monetization. The intent is
ecosystem positioning: be a canonical Google News server during the
formative months of the Model Context Protocol, so the project keeps
surfacing wherever a developer looks for an MCP tool. Distribution
across many registries (Smithery, mcp.so, Glama, PulseMCP, Awesome MCP
Servers, Archestra, and others) is the discovery strategy. The README
also carries JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` metadata and an `llms.txt`
guide so that AI agents themselves can read the server's identity,
parameters, and use cases without parsing marketing prose — the same
discovery-by-machine approach used across the rest of this portfolio.

**Technical approach:** TypeScript on Node.js, built against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` v1.x,
shipped as an ES-module CLI binary (`google-news-server`) declared in
`package.json`. Transport is stdio, so the server runs as a subprocess
the desktop AI client launches. HTTP calls go through axios; SerpAPI is
the underlying data source (2 requests/second, 5 concurrent, 1-hour
cache). On top of the raw API the server adds a thin intelligence
layer: parameter validation, topic categorization, multi-language
formatting, and error handling with clear messages.

The repo also ships production packaging — a Dockerfile, a Smithery
config, a `manifest.json`, a JSON-LD `structured-data.json`, an
`llms.txt`, and a downloadable `.mcpb` install bundle — plus a full
set of community-health docs (CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY, SUPPORT,
GOVERNANCE, CHANGELOG, issue/PR templates) added in 2026.

The third contribution is community, not code: **issue #76** in the
official `modelcontextprotocol/servers` repo (opened Nov 27 2024, two
days after MCP's launch) documented the Windows + NVM PATH failure that
stopped Claude Desktop from launching npm-based MCP servers. The fix —
point the client config at the absolute path of `node.exe` instead of
relying on PATH — is cited as the primary troubleshooting step in this
server's README.

**Outcomes:** The server is published on npm as `@chanmeng666/google-news-server`
and listed across multiple MCP discovery surfaces, with third-party
recognition:
- PulseMCP "Top Pick" badge *(external claim — needs registry check)*
- Glama A-grade license rating *(external claim — needs registry check)*
- Smithery one-command install, plus listings on mcp.so, Awesome MCP
  Servers, Archestra, and other catalogs *(exact catalog count is
  external — needs registry check)*
- Independent deep-dive write-ups by third-party reviewers (Skywork
  AI, AgentSkillsHub) *(external claim — needs registry check)*
- A hosted deployment listed on Fronteir AI *(external claim — needs
  registry check)*

Alongside it, the companion `server-google-jobs` (first commit late
December 2024) applies the same pattern to Google Jobs search
*(Jobs-repo specifics not verifiable from the News clone — see
flags)*. Issue #76 remains visible in the official MCP servers
repository as a record of an early ecosystem contribution.

**My role:** I built and maintain the server solo — the code, the README, the npm
publish, the registry submissions, the packaging, and the
issue-tracker responses. I also filed the early issue #76
troubleshooting contribution. Together these make the
early-MCP-developer story concrete: not a theoretical early adopter,
but someone who shipped, distributed, documented, and supported a
working MCP server within the protocol's first month.
### 2. echook (Echo + Hook) — AI-operated hooks plugin for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI — install, configure, and use with natural language only; grew from audio notifications into a context-window status line and richer session telemetry
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** 
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/echook
- **Tech stack:** Python 3.6+, Claude Code Plugin SDK, Cursor IDE 3.2.16+ third-party hooks, Codex CLI hooks API, hooks.json native matcher routing, NDJSON structured logging (schema audio-hooks.v1), ElevenLabs TTS API, Slack / Discord / Teams / ntfy webhooks, PowerShell MediaPlayer (Windows), afplay (macOS), mpg123 / ffplay / paplay / aplay (Linux auto-detect), GitHub Actions CI (Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Python 3.9/3.12/3.13), Remotion 4 (promo video), React 19 (promo video), Suno (background music generation)
- **Commits (solo):** 151 (2025-11-01 → 2026-07-16, ~8 months); effectively single-author
- **GitHub stars:** ~75 (echook, renamed from claude-code-audio-hooks)
- **Version:** v6.3.4 (plugin `audio-hooks` on the chanmeng-audio-hooks marketplace)
- **Hook events:** 37 canonical events (3 on by default), mapped per harness: Claude Code 37 · Cursor 18 native (8 via auto-bridge) · Codex 10
- **Harnesses:** 3 (Claude Code · Cursor IDE · Codex CLI) from one canonical source, CI-enforced no-drift
- **Runtime:** Python 3.6+ standard library only (no third-party runtime deps); distributed via plugin marketplaces + source, not npm
- **CLI:** ~40 JSON-in/JSON-out subcommands; a `manifest` command emits the entire surface (subcommands, hooks, config keys, error codes) as machine-readable JSON so an agent can self-orient
- **Status line:** 29 configurable segments (context-window color bands, quota bars, model/branch/cost), auto-reflowing to terminal width
- **Channels:** audio · desktop toast · TTS (speaks the agent's actual final message) · webhook (Slack/Discord/Teams/ntfy, versioned payload)
- **Tests / CI:** 248 unittest cases on a 3-OS × 3-Python matrix (Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × 3.9/3.12/3.13) + build-drift gate
**Summary:** An audio-and-status notification system for Claude Code, Cursor
IDE, and Codex CLI. The core idea: you never have to learn the
tool. You talk to your AI agent in plain English — "install
audio-hooks," "switch to chimes," "snooze for 30 minutes," "send
alerts to my Slack" — and the agent runs the commands, reads the
results, and reports back. It ships 37 hook events (3 on by
default), two audio themes, warnings before you hit your 5-hour
and 7-day usage limits, webhooks to Slack/Discord/Teams/ntfy,
text-to-speech that reads the agent's actual reply aloud, and a
29-segment status bar that tracks how much of the context window
is left. Now on v6.3.4 with 248 automated tests running on
Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS across Python 3.9–3.13. Open source
under MIT; runs on the Python standard library with no extra
dependencies. A sister repo, echook-promo-video, ships an
88-second product film built entirely in code.

**Impact:** A notification system for AI coding assistants — Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex — that you install and set up just by talking to the AI in plain English. It plays sounds, reads the agent's reply aloud, warns you before you hit usage limits, and shows a live status bar.

**Problem:** Developers running long AI coding sessions on Claude Code,
Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI have no easy way to *hear* what their
agent is doing. They alt-tab to check progress, miss
usage-limit warnings, and only notice a stuck session by
looking at it. Add-on notification tools exist, but they all
share the same flaw: the human sits downstream of the command
line. You have to read docs, memorize flags, copy commands, and
re-learn the same surface every time it changes. That breaks
down the moment a serious user rotates between three editors,
each with its own configuration language and its own muscle
memory. The real mismatch isn't audio — it's the working
contract between humans, AI agents, and developer tooling.

**Audience:** Software engineers operating Claude Code, Cursor IDE, or Codex
CLI at the "AI-first" level — people comfortable telling their
coding agent to install, theme, snooze, webhook, or reconfigure
things in plain English rather than memorizing subcommands.
Also: power users debugging long-running workflows where a
sound is faster than watching a screen; teams rolling out
shared notification defaults across several developers; and
researchers who want a working reference implementation of an
AI-first plugin contract.

**Product logic:** echook flips the usual relationship: the human is upstream, and
the AI agent learns the tool on the human's behalf. One install
prompt — *"install the audio-hooks plugin from
github.com/ChanMeng666/echook"* — starts the whole flow. The
agent runs `claude plugin marketplace add` and `claude plugin
install`, the user types `/reload-plugins` exactly once (the
only manual step in the tool's entire lifetime, because Claude
Code's reload has no command-line equivalent), and from then on
every interaction is a sentence. *"Switch to chimes." "Snooze
for 8 hours." "Send alerts to my Slack at this webhook and test
it." "Turn everything off except stop and permission
requests."* The `/audio-hooks` skill reads the intent, runs the
right `audio-hooks` command, reads its JSON output, and tells
you what happened. You memorize nothing. Cross-editor parity is
part of the same contract: every prompt works the same way on
Cursor's agent and on Codex CLI, because all three drive the
same Python program against the same JSON interface.

**Business logic:** Open source under MIT — no paywall, no premium tier, no
telemetry. The value is build-in-public credibility: echook is
the demonstration piece for Chan's AI-first product philosophy,
and it shows up in Engram-cohort interviews, recruiter
conversations, and the README of downstream client projects. A
sustained release cadence (now v6.3.4), a three-OS
continuous-integration matrix, and a 248-case test suite
guarding the cross-editor contract make it visibly production
engineering, not a weekend script. The Remotion promo-video
sister repo extends the same proof — it shows Chan can ship not
only the product but the marketing film for it, end to end and
entirely in code, with no video-production tooling.

**Technical approach:** AI-first design at every layer. The `audio-hooks` command-line
tool exposes a stable, JSON-only interface with roughly 40
subcommands, no prompts and no spinners, versioned NDJSON event
logging (schema `audio-hooks.v1`, 5 MB rotation × 3 files), and
a fixed set of error codes (`AUDIO_FILE_MISSING`,
`WEBHOOK_TIMEOUT`, `DUAL_INSTALL_DETECTED`, and so on) where
each error carries a `suggested_command` field so an agent can
recover on its own without scraping text. Hook coverage spans
37 canonical events — session start and end, tool execution,
permission requests, file changes, MCP elicitation, and
usage-limit warnings (5-hour and 7-day windows with configurable
80%/95% thresholds, each `(window, threshold, resets_at)` firing
exactly once via debounce markers). Two audio themes ship: an
ElevenLabs "Jessica" voice or modern UI chimes; rebuilds are
declarative via `config/audio_manifest.json` plus the ElevenLabs
generator. Webhooks fan out to Slack, Discord, Teams, and ntfy
through a fire-and-forget subprocess with a versioned
`audio-hooks.webhook.v1` payload, so they never block the hook.
The status bar offers 29 configurable segments, including
color-coded context-window and usage bars with absolute token
counts (e.g. `Context: 83% (166K/200K)`) so users see the
"agent dumb zone" before they reach it. The deepest piece is the
cross-editor architecture: canonical sources live in `/hooks/`,
`/bin/`, `/audio/`, `/cursor-hooks/`, and `/codex-hooks/`, and
`build-plugin.sh` syncs them into the Claude Code plugin layout
— one source of truth, three editor targets — with continuous
integration enforcing no-drift via `build-plugin.sh --check`.
Cursor rides its 3.2.16+ auto-bridge so most users get support
for free; Cursor-only installs use a native
`~/.cursor/hooks.json` registration guarded by a
`DUPLICATE_BRIDGE` error that aborts if both paths would run at
once. The Codex path handles OpenAI's required
`[features]\ncodex_hooks = true` flag by writing a fresh
`~/.codex/config.toml` when none exists and emitting
machine-readable `next_steps` for the agent to edit an existing
one — echook never round-trips a user's TOML, because that would
destroy comments and formatting. Canonical events with no Codex
equivalent no-op cleanly under the Codex invoker with a debug
log line, never blocking the session. The whole contract is
validated by 248 test cases across Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS on
Python 3.9, 3.12, and 3.13 in GitHub Actions.

**Outcomes:** ~76 GitHub stars (external — needs registry check; live 75),
151 commits across the 5.x → 6.x line (now v6.3.4), and 248
automated test cases. The defining release sequence: v5.0 (Jan
2026) shipped the AI-first redesign — JSON command interface,
`/audio-hooks` skill, NDJSON logging, plugin-native install;
v5.2.0 (May 2026) added native Codex CLI support with +33
contract tests; v5.2.1 (May 2026) executed the echook rebrand —
a "door-only" GitHub rename from `claude-code-audio-hooks` to
`echook` (leading with "Claude Code" had become misleading once
native Cursor and Codex paths shipped) that preserved every
existing install via GitHub's repo redirect and kept the
`audio-hooks` command name, the `chanmeng-audio-hooks`
marketplace name, and all state directories unchanged. The
Engram Architect Cohort Engagement Letter (May 9, 2026)
explicitly names *claude-code-audio-hooks* among Chan's
preserved IP — formal recognition by a commercial
agentic-infrastructure firm that this is portfolio-grade
engineering, not a hobby script. The sister repo
echook-promo-video then closed the loop on the thesis: an
88-second 1080p product film composed entirely in code (React +
Remotion, with Suno-generated background music and ElevenLabs
voice and effects — no video-editing suites and no human voice
actors), fully reproducible from two commands.

**My role:** Sole author of both repos. On echook proper: designed the
AI-first contract (JSON interface + NDJSON log + fixed error
codes + `suggested_command` recovery), wrote every hook-event
integration, engineered the three-editor synchronization
pipeline (canonical sources synced into the plugin layout via
`build-plugin.sh`, with CI enforcement), authored the Codex
TOML feature-flag bootstrap pattern, and executed the 5.2.1
rebrand without breaking compatibility. On echook-promo-video:
designed the nine-section narrative arc, built the reusable
visual primitives (TypewriterText, Terminal, SoundWave,
GreenGrid, CounterNumber, GlowText), orchestrated the Remotion
transitions, wrote the ElevenLabs audio-generation script, and
tuned the volume-ducking so the background music dips under
voiceover and demo playback. Maintains both repos today.
### 3. ArchLang — An original declarative floor-plan language that compiles .arch source into professional, dimensioned floor plans — 'Typst/LaTeX for architecture.' Zero-dependency, isomorphic TypeScript (hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser), errors returned as data not thrown, with architectural-soundness linting, human-circulation analysis, a deterministic repair pass, five export formats (SVG/DXF/PDF/PNG/text), an agent-native CLI, full LSP + VS Code extension, and a client-side playground. Published to npm; the compiler engine behind ArchCanvas.
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://playground.archlang.uk
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/archlang
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript 5.7 (ESM, zero runtime dependencies), Hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser + expression evaluator, tsup (build), Biome 2.5 (lint/format), Vitest 2.1 + fast-check (property tests) + pixelmatch (visual regression), Optional lazy add-ons: @resvg/resvg-js (PNG), clipper2-wasm (geometry), pdfkit (PDF), LSP (hover/completion/rename/signature), VS Code extension (ChanMeng.archlang, TextMate grammar), Vite + CodeMirror 6 playground (100% client-side), VitePress docs, GitHub Action (renders ```arch fences to images)
- **Commits (solo):** 304 (297 by Chan + 7 automated bumps) (2026-06-25 → 2026-07-15, ~3 weeks intensive); sole author
- **npm package:** @chanmeng666/archlang v1.18.0 (34 tagged releases, 15 ADRs)
- **Source:** 92 TypeScript files (~23.3k LOC) + 109 test files
- **Primitives:** 8 elements (wall · room · door · window · opening · furniture · dim · column) + fixture-symbol catalog
- **Export formats:** 5 — SVG + DXF (zero-dep) · PDF + PNG (lazily-loaded optional add-ons) · text-only plan
- **Diagnostics:** 83 distinct E_*/W_* codes, each carrying a byte span + a machine-applicable fix
- **Lint rules:** 8 architectural-soundness rule modules (reachability, door-swing collision, windowless bedroom, wet-room enclosure, circulation)
- **CLI:** ~20 subcommands, all with --json + deterministic exit codes (0 ok / 1 IO / 2 source / 3 usage)
- **Downstream:** the compiler engine behind ArchCanvas, which consumes frozen-API contracts (data-arch-id anchors, diffPlans()) verbatim
**Summary:** ArchLang is a small language for floor plans: you describe walls, rooms, doors, windows, and furniture in plain text, and it compiles to a precise, dimensioned drawing — SVG, DXF, PDF, PNG, or a text-only plan. The same source always produces the identical drawing, so widening a bedroom is a one-number edit instead of re-drawing. It also checks whether a plan is actually livable (can you reach the bathroom? does a bedroom have a window?), and is the floor-plan engine behind the AI design agent ArchCanvas.

**Impact:** Describe a floor plan in plain text — walls, rooms, doors, windows — and get a precise, dimensioned architectural drawing back. Change one number and exactly one thing moves. It also checks that a plan is actually livable, and powers ArchCanvas, an AI design agent.

**Problem:** Floor plans live in two unsatisfying states: hand-drawn in heavy CAD tools, or generated as raster images. Both make the thing you most want to do — a precise, parametric edit — expensive: widening a bedroom means re-drawing, or re-rolling an image and hoping the rest survives. Neither is authorable by an AI agent, because there is no deterministic text representation an agent can write, verify, and tweak. And an image never *knows* whether the plan it depicts is actually habitable — whether a bathroom is reachable only through a bedroom, whether a door swings into another door, whether a bedroom has a window. ArchLang exists to make a floor plan a **program**: exact, deterministic, diff-able, agent-authorable, and checkable for architectural soundness.

**Audience:** Three audiences. Architects and self-builders who want plans that are precise and reproducible rather than hand-drawn approximations; design-tool builders who need a floor-plan engine they can embed (ArchCanvas is the first); and AI agents that need to author and verify plans without ever rendering an image — for which ArchLang exposes its entire language in one ~2k-token spec page, returns a plan "as facts" (rooms, areas, adjacency, door connections, circulation) over `describe --json`, and scores a plan against a written brief with `validate --intent`.

**Product logic:** You write `.arch` source. The coordinate model is integer millimetres with an SVG-convention origin; elements are placed either absolutely (`room 4000x3000 at (0,0)`) or **relationally** — `right-of`, `left-of`, `above`, `below` with `align` and `gap`, resolved by deterministic topological arithmetic so the layout stays consistent under edits. The language is genuinely programmable: `let` bindings, pure value-functions, `for`/`if`/`while`, arrays, string interpolation, optional metric unit suffixes (`4m`/`40cm`), and `import` + `component` for reuse — all expand-time and pure (no runtime, clock, or I/O), so output is byte-for-byte deterministic.

Compilation is a returned value, not a side effect: `compile()` hands back `{ svg, errors, warnings, diagnostics, ast, scene }` and **never throws** on a bad plan — every diagnostic carries a byte span and a machine-applicable `fix`, and the SVG is simply left empty. On top of the compiler sit several analysis surfaces: a **lint** pass over eight architectural-soundness rules (reachability, door-swing collision, windowless bedroom, unenclosed wet room, too-narrow door…); a **circulation** analysis that models how a person actually walks the plan on a clearance-eroded nav grid (per-room walk distance, narrowest pinch, path circuitousness) with an optional route overlay; a deterministic **repair** pass that nudges furniture out of walls and door swings and emits a change log; an **intent** check that scores the plan against a written brief and returns a pass/fail exit code; and a **suggest** surface that emits candidate door/window statements to fix reachability problems. All of it is reachable from a ~20-subcommand CLI where every command supports `--json` and returns deterministic exit codes, plus an LSP, a VS Code extension, four render themes, accessibility annotations, and a fully client-side playground.

**Business logic:** ArchLang is free and open-source, MIT, published on npm as `@chanmeng666/archlang`. Its strategic role is twofold. As an artifact it is a credential — an original programming language with a hand-written compiler, an 83-code diagnostic system, domain-specific static analysis, and full IDE tooling, built and versioned with engineering discipline (15 ADRs, 34 tagged releases). As infrastructure it is the **engine that makes a commercial product possible**: ArchCanvas (the AI design agent) depends on it, writing parametric ArchLang rather than freehand SVG so that its select-to-act editing, parametric sliders, and semantic diffs all work — capabilities that only exist because the underlying plan is a deterministic program. Agent access is designed CLI-first: a CLI costs zero context until it is called, whereas an MCP tool schema sits in an agent's window permanently. ArchLang still publishes an optional MCP server (`@chanmeng666/archlang-mcp`, on the official registry) so MCP-native hosts can discover it, but keeps the SDK quarantined in that one package so the core stays zero-dependency.

**Technical approach:** A pure-TypeScript, zero-runtime-dependency compiler: a hand-written lexer (line/col tracking), a recursive-descent parser that recovers rather than throwing, and a separate expression evaluator, all isomorphic so the same code runs in Node and in the browser — the playground sends nothing to a server. The heavy or native pieces (resvg for PNG raster, clipper2-wasm for polygon geometry, pdfkit for PDF) are **optional and lazily imported** behind bundler-ignore guards, so a browser bundle never tries to resolve them and a default install pulls none of them. Output determinism is enforced with property tests (fast-check) and visual-regression tests (pixelmatch). The diagnostic layer — 83 stable `E_*`/`W_*` codes, each with a byte span and a suggested fix, surfaced as data and even as a self-describing error-SVG card — is the deliberate foundation of the agent story: an LLM can author a plan, read the structured errors, and self-correct in a tight loop without a human or an image in the middle.

**Outcomes:** A complete, published language: 304 commits (297 mine, the rest automated dependency bumps) over roughly three weeks of intensive development (from 2026-06-25), across 92 source files (~23.3k LOC) against 109 test files, released to npm at v1.18.0 over 34 tagged releases with 15 architecture decision records. The surface area is unusually complete for a solo language project — 8 primitives, 5 export formats, 8 lint-rule modules, a circulation analyzer, a repair pass, an intent-scoring channel, a ~20-command CLI, a full LSP, a VS Code extension, an optional MCP server, a client-side playground, a docs site, a published HuggingFace dataset of self-verifying repair trajectories, and a GitHub Action that renders every ```arch fence in a repo's Markdown. Most tellingly, it is already load-bearing: ArchCanvas's in-browser use of the core drove real hardening, proving the engine under a downstream product rather than in isolation.

**My role:** I invented the language and wrote everything — the language design, the hand-written lexer / parser / evaluator, the 83-code diagnostic system, all five export backends, the lint / circulation / repair / intent / suggest pipelines, the LSP and VS Code extension, the CodeMirror playground, the docs site, the MCP server, and the npm packaging. 297 of the 304 commits are mine; the remaining 7 are automated dependency bumps.
### 4. ArchCanvas — 'Your AI architect — dimensioned floor plans you can actually build from.' An AI design agent that turns a natural-language brief into ONE strong floor plan — written as parametric ArchLang (Chan's own DSL), compiled to real dimensioned SVG, plus a GPT-Image rendering grounded on that exact plan — then refined conversationally on an infinite zoomable canvas where edits morph in place. Select-to-act editing, a review mode that overlays circulation and pins critique, parametric sliders, and export of the whole design history as a real git repo. Refinement mutates the plan in place — it never regenerates it.
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://archcanvas.uk/
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15.3 (App Router) + React 19 + TypeScript 5.8, Vercel AI SDK (ai 4.3, @ai-sdk/openai) + OpenAI 6.45 — orchestrator migrated off GPT-4o to a newer reasoning model for tool use; GPT-4o retained for vision, GPT Image grounded renderings (view-aware model chain: aerial vs eye-level), @chanmeng666/archlang ^1.18.0 (Chan's own declarative floor-plan compiler, consumed as a package), Neon Postgres + Drizzle ORM (9 tables, schema-push), Stripe 17 (credits ledger, dual live/test mode), Cloudinary (rendering storage), react-zoom-pan-pinch (infinite zoomable canvas), isomorphic-git + memfs + fflate (export design history as a real git repo, in-memory), jose (JWT/JWKS auth), Zod validation · shadcn/ui + Tailwind 4 + Framer Motion + GSAP, Docker canary-then-swap deploy over SSH (DigitalOcean VPS, Traefik + Let's Encrypt, Cloudflare Full-Strict)
- **Commits (solo):** ~295 (268 non-merge) (2026-04-08 → 2026-07-16, ~3 months); sole author
- **Surfaces:** 13 pages · 28 API route handlers · 71 components · 205 source files
- **Database:** 9 Drizzle tables on Neon (projects · designFrames · agentThreads · credits · creditTransactions · shares · generationAttempts · uploads · stripeWebhookEvents)
- **Agent tools:** 9 (7 user-facing: askDesignQuestions · generateFloorPlanDSL[new plan] · editFloorPlanDSL[minimal diff] · generateRendering · critiquePlan · analyzeReference[vision] · analyzeFloorPlan)
- **Generation unit:** one generation = plan + grounded rendering + critique, produced as a single atomic set; edits, parametric tweaks, re-renders, and review mode all operate on that set without re-generating it
- **Git export:** in-memory isomorphic-git repo (memfs), one commit per design version — the commit log replays the design conversation (cap 200 commits / 25 MB)
- **Deploy:** private repo → GitHub Actions → GHCR → canary-then-swap over SSH (boot canary, poll /api/health, swap only if healthy) on a DO VPS behind Traefik + Cloudflare
**Summary:** ArchCanvas is an AI design agent for architects and self-builders. Describe a building in plain language and it asks a few questions, then designs one dimensioned floor plan you can actually build from — with room labels and measurements — alongside a realistic AI rendering of it. From there it stays conversational: on an infinite zoomable canvas you refine the plan by talking to it, scrub through every past version, and export the whole design as files or as a full change history.

**Impact:** An AI design agent for architects and self-builders: describe a building in plain words and it designs a dimensioned floor plan you can actually build from, plus a realistic rendering — then you refine it by talking, on an infinite zoomable canvas.

**Problem:** AI floor-plan tools produce pretty pictures you cannot build from and cannot precisely edit. An image-generated plan has no real dimensions, no guarantee a wall is straight, and no handle to grab when you want the kitchen two metres wider — you re-prompt and hope. Meanwhile professional CAD is powerful but slow and expert-only, and neither world lets you simply *talk* to your design. ArchCanvas closes that gap: a plan you can converse your way to, that is nonetheless a real, dimensioned, buildable artifact you can keep editing precisely.

**Audience:** Architects and designers who want to move fast from brief to a credible dimensioned plan, and self-builders / renovators who have a vision but not CAD skills and need something they (or their builder) can actually work from. Because every plan is a real floor-plan program underneath, the same product serves a careful professional nudging a wall thickness with a slider and a first-timer who just wants to describe their dream house and see it — and refine it — in plain language.

**Product logic:** The studio runs in two acts. **Act I** is a full-screen briefing conversation: the user picks a typology (residential, accessible clinic, open-plan office, passive house) and describes the build; the agent asks two-to-five clarifying questions inline, then generates ONE plan — deliberately not a grid of options — as parametric ArchLang, compiles it to a dimensioned SVG with `data-arch-id` anchors, renders a grounded AI visualization, and critiques it. That single turn is the only point at which the agent is asked for a brand-new plan.

**Act II** is a canvas-first studio, and nothing in it regenerates. Edits are conversational and **morph the plan in place** — the parent geometry fades to a dashed ghost, a semantic-diff chip shows what changed, and a version-lineage timeline lets you scrub the history. **Select-to-act**: click a room, door, or window and a floating action bar offers verb chips (Enlarge / Shrink / Widen / Move) plus agent-suggested scoped edits. A **Parametric Tweaks** panel turns the plan's own variables (room sizes, counts, wall thickness) into live sliders that recompile instantly with no AI call at all. **Review mode** toggles a walking-circulation overlay (routes, pinch points) and pins anchored critique findings, each with a one-click "have AI fix" — display-only, never auto-applied. The finished design exports as SVG/PNG/DXF/PDF, or as a full versioned change history whose log *is* the design conversation.

**Business logic:** The unit of work is one generation turn — plan + grounded rendering + critique, produced as a single atomic set — and everything downstream of it is refinement rather than regeneration. That split is deliberate: a design task's value is lumpy (you want a few strong plans, then iterate on them for a long time), so every edit, parametric tweak, re-render, and review action mutates the existing frame in place, and the model is re-invoked only when the user genuinely asks for new work. The advantage is the ArchLang pairing: because plans are a deterministic program rather than pixels, ArchCanvas can offer editing, tweaking, diffing, and change-history export that an image-generation competitor structurally cannot.

**Technical approach:** A single Next.js 15 monolith (React 19, TypeScript 5.8) over a 9-table Drizzle schema on Neon Postgres, with Stripe credits, Cloudinary rendering storage, and jose-based auth. The AI layer is an orchestrated tool-agent on the Vercel AI SDK: the orchestrator was deliberately migrated off GPT-4o (which under-used tools and wrote non-parametric DSL) to **gpt-5.5**, while GPT-4o is retained for vision (reference-image analysis). Its tools center on `generateFloorPlanDSL` — write parametric ArchLang, compile it through the `@chanmeng666/archlang` package (now 1.18), pass a ship-gate, save a frame — with `editFloorPlanDSL` as the minimal-diff follow-up. Renderings are a **grounded, view-aware chain**: the compiled plan is rasterized and sent as the grounding image to GPT Image 2, with model order chosen per view (aerial vs eye-level) so the render matches the real geometry instead of hallucinating a layout. `designFrames` stores each version's ArchLang source, compiled SVG, rendering URLs, lint warnings, review findings, and a self-referential `parentFrameId` for lineage — which is exactly what the in-memory change-history export (isomorphic-git + memfs, zipped with fflate) walks root-to-tip. Deployment is a canary-then-swap over SSH: build a Docker image, push to GHCR, boot a health-checked canary, and swap traffic only if `/api/health` passes — otherwise abort untouched.

**Outcomes:** Live in production at archcanvas.uk, solo-built across roughly 295 commits over three months (2026-04-08 → 2026-07-16): 13 pages, 28 API route handlers, 71 components, and 205 source files over a 9-table schema. The product is unusually rigorous about testing its own thesis — a `generationAttempts` telemetry table tracks first-pass ArchLang validity per model, and a `bench` harness runs briefs through both the DSL pipeline and a legacy freehand-pixel pipeline as a control group (now with timing and token axes), so the "DSL beats pixels" claim is measured, not asserted. It is also the proving ground that hardened ArchLang: real in-browser use of the compiler drove concrete upstream fixes, most recently the 1.18 topology work.

**My role:** Sole author of the entire product — the two-act studio and canvas UX, the AI orchestrator and its tools, the grounded view-aware rendering chain, the ArchLang integration, the 9-table schema and credit/billing system, the change-history export, the falsifiability instruments, and the canary-then-swap deployment. And of ArchLang itself, the floor-plan language it is built on. Every commit is mine.
### 5. Vitex — AI Career Agent (vitex.org.nz) — Agent-ready AI career agent (vitex.org.nz) — turns a job description + free-text background into a tailored, ATS-optimized resume PDF + cover letter in ~30s via an 8-step pipeline. The whole product is reachable over an authenticated public HTTP API, plus a vitex-cli and a stdio MCP server (Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor); a DB partial-unique index makes each generation idempotent, so an SSE reconnect or a concurrent retry never re-runs the pipeline. Vercel AI SDK + gpt-5.5 (reasoning) / gpt-5.4-mini (extract) + Typst local compile (<100ms), on a DigitalOcean VPS via Docker + Traefik + GitHub Actions
[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://www.vitex.org.nz/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/easy-resume
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript 5, Vercel AI SDK v6, @ai-sdk/openai, OpenAI gpt-5.5 (reasoning) / gpt-5.4-mini (extract), Zod schemas, Typst (local binary), Neon Postgres, Drizzle ORM, Upstash Redis, Stack Auth, Stripe, Cloudinary, shadcn/ui + Phantom design system, Framer Motion, Public v1 HTTP API (OpenAPI spec + curl playbook in skill.md), vitex-cli (thin API client, bin `vitex`), MCP server (`vitex mcp` via npx, stdio — Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor), Docker, Traefik, DigitalOcean VPS, GitHub Actions CI/CD
- **Commits (solo):** 379 (2025-01-11 → 2026-07-14, ~18 months); essentially sole author
- **Surfaces:** 11 pages · 30 API route handlers · 25 components · 232 source files (~30k LOC)
- **Pipeline:** 8 steps (parse JD ∥ parse background → match → tailor + faithfulness gate → ATS score ∥ cover letter → render → Typst compile)
- **Templates:** 7 AI-auto-selected (academic · banking · compact · creative · executive · modern-cv · two-column)
- **Agent surfaces:** one core, N thin adapters — web SSE + public REST v1 (API key) + hosted OAuth-protected MCP + published vitex-cli with a stdio MCP; 9 MCP tools; full OAuth 2.1 AS with dynamic client registration + PKCE
- **Idempotency:** a generation is committed exactly once, and only after a PDF actually compiles; a DB partial-unique index survives SSE reconnects + concurrent races, so retries and refinements never duplicate a run
- **Models:** two-tier routing (cheap extract tier + reasoning tier, env-overridable); deterministic no-LLM ATS scoring + anti-hallucination faithfulness gate
- **PDF engine:** local Typst binary compile <100ms, SHA-256-cached; no external document API
- **Tests:** 49 test files / 437 tests
**Summary:** AI career agent live at **vitex.org.nz**. Paste a job ad and describe your background in plain text, and an 8-step AI pipeline writes a resume and cover letter tailored to that specific job, then compiles them into a polished, ATS-ready PDF in about 30 seconds across 7 auto-selected templates — no document is rendered by an outside service. Vitex is **agent-ready**: everything the website does is also reachable over a public API, a command-line tool, and an MCP server, so a terminal or an AI assistant (Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT) can drive the whole product without a browser.

**Impact:** Paste a job ad and describe your background — it writes a resume and cover letter tailored to that job and builds them into a polished PDF in about 30 seconds. Works from a browser, the command line, or inside an AI assistant.

**Problem:** Most AI resume tools fail in one of two ways: (a) a thin chatbot wrapper that hands you a paragraph and calls it a resume — no PDF, no ATS-aware structure, no template intelligence; or (b) a form-filling site that spends a minute-plus rendering the same generic template every other user gets. Both miss the real job: the resume has to be tailored to the *specific* job ad, the document has to survive automated applicant-tracking (ATS) parsing, the visual template has to fit the target industry, and the whole round-trip has to feel instant. None of that is true of "paste your CV into ChatGPT."

**Audience:** Three groups. **Job seekers in live applications** — especially in the NZ / Australia / Asia-Pacific markets where Chan personally needed the tool and where vitex.org.nz deliberately lives. **Career-changers** who have to re-tailor every application — the people for whom rewriting a resume per job ad is the actual bottleneck. And **AI-engineering reviewers** assessing Chan's ability to ship a production multi-step AI agent with real users, real billing, and real operations — not a demo.

**Product logic:** The flow is deliberately short. (1) **Homepage** — paste a job ad and describe your background in free text; no CV upload, no field-by-field form. (2) **Generate** — click run and the 8-step pipeline streams its progress live (reading the job ad → reading your background → matching → tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter → building the PDF), so you watch each stage finish instead of staring at a spinner, then land in the **Editor** with a PDF preview, an ATS score with concrete feedback, and the matching cover letter. (3) **Refine & export** — a plain-English box ("make the leadership section stronger", "shorten the intro") re-does just that slice rather than regenerating everything; then download the PDF or publish a share link. A dashboard keeps a searchable history for signed-in users.

**Business logic:** Each run is a multi-call AI pipeline plus a document compile, so capacity is metered per successful result rather than left open-ended: the ledger records a run **exactly once, and only after a PDF actually compiles**, which makes a failed or retried attempt a non-event. Public **share links** give the product organic reach — a candidate who lands a job can credit Vitex, and the shared resume is itself a live demo of what the product produces. The full stack (auth, database, cache, payments, AI, PDF rendering) is shaped for a real user base, not a showcase.

**Technical approach:** **AI agent layer.** The pipeline runs on the **Vercel AI SDK** with the OpenAI provider across **eight sequential stages**, on two-tier model routing — a cheaper **extract** model (default `gpt-5.4-mini`) for parsing and a stronger **reasoning** model (default `gpt-5.5`) for tailoring, both overridable per deployment, with per-tier reasoning-effort control. Each stage emits a **Zod-validated** structured output:
1. **JD parsing** — role, seniority, must-have and nice-to-have skills, company signals
2. **Background parsing** — free-text description → structured skills/experience
3. **Match analysis** — candidate↔job fit profile
4. **Tailoring** — reorder, rewrite, and emphasise history toward the parsed job ad
5. **ATS scoring** — a **deterministic, no-LLM** check of parse-ability, with actionable feedback
6. **Cover letter** — generated from the same parsed pair
7. **Render** — auto-select a template + seeded design tokens, emit Typst source
8. **Compile** — Typst → PDF server-side; the PDF is the deliverable, so the run is committed only here

Right after tailoring, a deterministic **faithfulness gate** re-checks for fabricated skills or employers and triggers one bounded corrective re-tailor — an anti-hallucination guard with no model in the check itself. Every stage streams progress over **Server-Sent Events**. Inputs are read from `sessionStorage` rather than URL params so deep links can't leak a user's data.

**Document engine — Typst, not LaTeX.** PDFs are compiled by a **local Typst binary** inside the Docker image, with no call to any outside service; the project migrated from LaTeX to Typst on 2026-04-06 (same day as the Cloudflare Workers → DigitalOcean VPS move) because Typst compiles in milliseconds where LaTeX needs seconds and Workers couldn't run the binary at all. The image pre-warms the Typst package cache (FontAwesome) so cold starts don't pay a fetch cost. **7 templates** (academic, banking, compact, creative, executive, modern-cv, two-column) are auto-selected from parsed industry/role signals.

**Persistence + product stack.** Neon serverless Postgres + Drizzle ORM (resumes, history, credit ledger); Upstash Redis for short-lived cache; Stack Auth for sign-in and agent API keys; Stripe for the credit ledger; Cloudinary for uploads and rendered PDFs; nanoid for share tokens.

**UI — Phantom.** Next.js 15 App Router + React 19 + TypeScript. The **Phantom** design system — a soft, flat, single-hue aubergine-and-lavender look with light-weight type and generous whitespace — is the deliberate visual identity, and replaced an earlier Neobrutalism (thick borders, hard shadows) treatment in a 2026 UI pass. shadcn/ui + Tailwind for primitives, Framer Motion for transitions, react-hook-form + Zod for validation.

**Deployment + hardening.** Production runs as a single **Docker container** on a **DigitalOcean VPS** behind **Traefik** (reverse proxy + automatic TLS), shipped by **GitHub Actions** with **health-gated rolling deploys** and automatic database migrations. Recent hardening added site-wide security headers (Content-Security-Policy + HSTS in production), request rate-limiting on the API-key/credit/billing routes, and auth + rate-limiting + sandboxing on the PDF-compile endpoint. Two earlier platform migrations (Railway → CF Workers → VPS) and the LaTeX → Typst swap all shipped without an outage.

**Outcomes:** Live and operational at **vitex.org.nz**, serving real users end-to-end from a pasted job ad to a compiled PDF. **379 commits over ~18 months** (2025-01-11 → 2026-07-14), essentially sole-authored. Two platform migrations and a LaTeX → Typst engine swap shipped without a service outage, each step captured in the commit log (the `xz-utils`/`curl` Dockerfile fixes, FontAwesome package pre-cache, the OpenAI strict-JSON-schema regression, the `sessionStorage` move for sensitive input). A 2026 UI pass replaced Neobrutalism with the Phantom design system; a subsequent security/ops wave added production CSP + HSTS, rate limits on the billing and API-key routes, a sandboxed compile endpoint, health-gated rolling deploys, and a production runbook. Backed by **49 test files / 437 tests**.

**My role:** Sole engineer, sole designer, sole product owner. Designed the 8-step agent pipeline with Zod-validated stages (migrating the editor from CopilotKit to the Vercel AI SDK agent chat along the way), wrote every API route, built the Stripe credit system, set up Stack Auth, designed the Drizzle schema for resumes / credits / share tokens, ran both platform migrations (Railway → CF Workers → DigitalOcean VPS), executed the LaTeX → Typst engine swap (including the Dockerfile fixes and the FontAwesome package pre-cache), designed the Phantom UI that replaced the earlier Neobrutalism treatment, built the plain-English refinement loop in the Editor, upgraded the model routing to gpt-5.5 / gpt-5.4-mini with per-tier reasoning-effort control, and hardened production (CSP + HSTS, rate limits, sandboxed compile, health-gated rolling deploys). I continue to operate the live service.

## Notable Open Source Projects

### Google News MCP Server — Early-ecosystem MCP server (shipped 5 weeks after Anthropic's Nov 25 2024 MCP launch) — PulseMCP "Top Pick", featured in Skywork AI deep-dive review, distributed across 15+ MCP catalogs
[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/server-google-news
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript, Node.js, @modelcontextprotocol/sdk, stdio transport, SerpAPI, axios, ES modules, npm (scoped @chanmeng666)

**Summary:** An early Model Context Protocol server — the open standard Anthropic
launched in November 2024 to connect AI assistants to outside tools.
Its first commit landed 35 days after that launch. It lets assistants
like Claude, Cursor, and Cline search live Google News through SerpAPI,
automatically sorted into topics (technology, business, science,
health) and offered in 10 languages. Published on npm as
`@chanmeng666/google-news-server`, it carries a PulseMCP "Top Pick"
badge and ships a one-click install bundle for Claude Desktop.

**Impact:** Lets AI assistants like Claude search live Google News — sorted into topics, in 10 languages. One of the first tools built for Anthropic's Model Context Protocol: shipped 35 days after the standard launched, and it's now listed across 15+ MCP directories.

**Problem:** Anthropic published the Model Context Protocol in November 2024 — the
open standard that lets AI assistants like Claude reach outside their
training data to real tools and live sources. In the first weeks after
launch the ecosystem had only a handful of reference servers, and none
of them let an assistant search current news. That was a concrete gap:
people were already asking their AI assistant "what's happening with X
today," a question no model can answer from training data alone, and
there was no Google News option in the MCP catalog.

A second friction appeared at the same time: Windows users running Node
Version Manager couldn't get *any* npm-based MCP server to connect to
Claude Desktop, because PATH resolution through NVM's shim layer broke
the desktop client's ability to launch the server process. That blocked
a large share of early Windows adopters from using MCP at all.

**Audience:** AI assistants and the developers wiring them up. Direct consumers:
Claude Desktop, Cursor's agent mode, Cline, Continue, and any
MCP-compliant client whose setup includes news search. Downstream: the
people asking those assistants about current events — analysts and
journalists monitoring coverage, developers building research and
content agents. The project is deliberately listed on multiple MCP
registries because that is how AI developers actually find servers —
they browse catalogs, not GitHub search.

**Product logic:** A user asks their AI assistant something like "what's happening with
[topic] in [region] this week." The assistant calls the server's one
tool, `google_news_search`, with a query plus optional `gl` (country)
and `hl` (language) codes. The server passes the request to SerpAPI's
Google News engine, gets structured results back, sorts them into
topics (AI & Technology, Business, Science & Research, Healthcare,
General) with lightweight pattern matching, and returns clean output
with each article's source, date, snippet, and link intact. It also
supports search by news topic, publisher, full-story coverage, and
section via token parameters, and falls back to English for
unsupported language codes.

The server runs over MCP's stdio channel — the default for desktop AI
clients — and installs in one command through Smithery's CLI (`npx -y
@smithery/cli install @chanmeng666/google-news-server --client
claude`), or via a packaged `.mcpb` bundle for one-click setup in
Claude Desktop.

**Business logic:** Open source, MIT licensed, no direct monetization. The intent is
ecosystem positioning: be a canonical Google News server during the
formative months of the Model Context Protocol, so the project keeps
surfacing wherever a developer looks for an MCP tool. Distribution
across many registries (Smithery, mcp.so, Glama, PulseMCP, Awesome MCP
Servers, Archestra, and others) is the discovery strategy. The README
also carries JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` metadata and an `llms.txt`
guide so that AI agents themselves can read the server's identity,
parameters, and use cases without parsing marketing prose — the same
discovery-by-machine approach used across the rest of this portfolio.

**Technical approach:** TypeScript on Node.js, built against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` v1.x,
shipped as an ES-module CLI binary (`google-news-server`) declared in
`package.json`. Transport is stdio, so the server runs as a subprocess
the desktop AI client launches. HTTP calls go through axios; SerpAPI is
the underlying data source (2 requests/second, 5 concurrent, 1-hour
cache). On top of the raw API the server adds a thin intelligence
layer: parameter validation, topic categorization, multi-language
formatting, and error handling with clear messages.

The repo also ships production packaging — a Dockerfile, a Smithery
config, a `manifest.json`, a JSON-LD `structured-data.json`, an
`llms.txt`, and a downloadable `.mcpb` install bundle — plus a full
set of community-health docs (CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY, SUPPORT,
GOVERNANCE, CHANGELOG, issue/PR templates) added in 2026.

The third contribution is community, not code: **issue #76** in the
official `modelcontextprotocol/servers` repo (opened Nov 27 2024, two
days after MCP's launch) documented the Windows + NVM PATH failure that
stopped Claude Desktop from launching npm-based MCP servers. The fix —
point the client config at the absolute path of `node.exe` instead of
relying on PATH — is cited as the primary troubleshooting step in this
server's README.

**Outcomes:** The server is published on npm as `@chanmeng666/google-news-server`
and listed across multiple MCP discovery surfaces, with third-party
recognition:
- PulseMCP "Top Pick" badge *(external claim — needs registry check)*
- Glama A-grade license rating *(external claim — needs registry check)*
- Smithery one-command install, plus listings on mcp.so, Awesome MCP
  Servers, Archestra, and other catalogs *(exact catalog count is
  external — needs registry check)*
- Independent deep-dive write-ups by third-party reviewers (Skywork
  AI, AgentSkillsHub) *(external claim — needs registry check)*
- A hosted deployment listed on Fronteir AI *(external claim — needs
  registry check)*

Alongside it, the companion `server-google-jobs` (first commit late
December 2024) applies the same pattern to Google Jobs search
*(Jobs-repo specifics not verifiable from the News clone — see
flags)*. Issue #76 remains visible in the official MCP servers
repository as a record of an early ecosystem contribution.

**My role:** I built and maintain the server solo — the code, the README, the npm
publish, the registry submissions, the packaging, and the
issue-tracker responses. I also filed the early issue #76
troubleshooting contribution. Together these make the
early-MCP-developer story concrete: not a theoretical early adopter,
but someone who shipped, distributed, documented, and supported a
working MCP server within the protocol's first month.
### echook (Echo + Hook) — AI-operated hooks plugin for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI — install, configure, and use with natural language only; grew from audio notifications into a context-window status line and richer session telemetry
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-17]
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/echook
- **Tech stack:** Python 3.6+, Claude Code Plugin SDK, Cursor IDE 3.2.16+ third-party hooks, Codex CLI hooks API, hooks.json native matcher routing, NDJSON structured logging (schema audio-hooks.v1), ElevenLabs TTS API, Slack / Discord / Teams / ntfy webhooks, PowerShell MediaPlayer (Windows), afplay (macOS), mpg123 / ffplay / paplay / aplay (Linux auto-detect), GitHub Actions CI (Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Python 3.9/3.12/3.13), Remotion 4 (promo video), React 19 (promo video), Suno (background music generation)

**Summary:** An audio-and-status notification system for Claude Code, Cursor
IDE, and Codex CLI. The core idea: you never have to learn the
tool. You talk to your AI agent in plain English — "install
audio-hooks," "switch to chimes," "snooze for 30 minutes," "send
alerts to my Slack" — and the agent runs the commands, reads the
results, and reports back. It ships 37 hook events (3 on by
default), two audio themes, warnings before you hit your 5-hour
and 7-day usage limits, webhooks to Slack/Discord/Teams/ntfy,
text-to-speech that reads the agent's actual reply aloud, and a
29-segment status bar that tracks how much of the context window
is left. Now on v6.3.4 with 248 automated tests running on
Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS across Python 3.9–3.13. Open source
under MIT; runs on the Python standard library with no extra
dependencies. A sister repo, echook-promo-video, ships an
88-second product film built entirely in code.

**Impact:** A notification system for AI coding assistants — Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex — that you install and set up just by talking to the AI in plain English. It plays sounds, reads the agent's reply aloud, warns you before you hit usage limits, and shows a live status bar.

**Problem:** Developers running long AI coding sessions on Claude Code,
Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI have no easy way to *hear* what their
agent is doing. They alt-tab to check progress, miss
usage-limit warnings, and only notice a stuck session by
looking at it. Add-on notification tools exist, but they all
share the same flaw: the human sits downstream of the command
