line. You have to read docs, memorize flags, copy commands, and
re-learn the same surface every time it changes. That breaks
down the moment a serious user rotates between three editors,
each with its own configuration language and its own muscle
memory. The real mismatch isn't audio — it's the working
contract between humans, AI agents, and developer tooling.

**Audience:** Software engineers operating Claude Code, Cursor IDE, or Codex
CLI at the "AI-first" level — people comfortable telling their
coding agent to install, theme, snooze, webhook, or reconfigure
things in plain English rather than memorizing subcommands.
Also: power users debugging long-running workflows where a
sound is faster than watching a screen; teams rolling out
shared notification defaults across several developers; and
researchers who want a working reference implementation of an
AI-first plugin contract.

**Product logic:** echook flips the usual relationship: the human is upstream, and
the AI agent learns the tool on the human's behalf. One install
prompt — *"install the audio-hooks plugin from
github.com/ChanMeng666/echook"* — starts the whole flow. The
agent runs `claude plugin marketplace add` and `claude plugin
install`, the user types `/reload-plugins` exactly once (the
only manual step in the tool's entire lifetime, because Claude
Code's reload has no command-line equivalent), and from then on
every interaction is a sentence. *"Switch to chimes." "Snooze
for 8 hours." "Send alerts to my Slack at this webhook and test
it." "Turn everything off except stop and permission
requests."* The `/audio-hooks` skill reads the intent, runs the
right `audio-hooks` command, reads its JSON output, and tells
you what happened. You memorize nothing. Cross-editor parity is
part of the same contract: every prompt works the same way on
Cursor's agent and on Codex CLI, because all three drive the
same Python program against the same JSON interface.

**Business logic:** Open source under MIT — no paywall, no premium tier, no
telemetry. The value is build-in-public credibility: echook is
the demonstration piece for Chan's AI-first product philosophy,
and it shows up in Engram-cohort interviews, recruiter
conversations, and the README of downstream client projects. A
sustained release cadence (now v6.3.4), a three-OS
continuous-integration matrix, and a 248-case test suite
guarding the cross-editor contract make it visibly production
engineering, not a weekend script. The Remotion promo-video
sister repo extends the same proof — it shows Chan can ship not
only the product but the marketing film for it, end to end and
entirely in code, with no video-production tooling.

**Technical approach:** AI-first design at every layer. The `audio-hooks` command-line
tool exposes a stable, JSON-only interface with roughly 40
subcommands, no prompts and no spinners, versioned NDJSON event
logging (schema `audio-hooks.v1`, 5 MB rotation × 3 files), and
a fixed set of error codes (`AUDIO_FILE_MISSING`,
`WEBHOOK_TIMEOUT`, `DUAL_INSTALL_DETECTED`, and so on) where
each error carries a `suggested_command` field so an agent can
recover on its own without scraping text. Hook coverage spans
37 canonical events — session start and end, tool execution,
permission requests, file changes, MCP elicitation, and
usage-limit warnings (5-hour and 7-day windows with configurable
80%/95% thresholds, each `(window, threshold, resets_at)` firing
exactly once via debounce markers). Two audio themes ship: an
ElevenLabs "Jessica" voice or modern UI chimes; rebuilds are
declarative via `config/audio_manifest.json` plus the ElevenLabs
generator. Webhooks fan out to Slack, Discord, Teams, and ntfy
through a fire-and-forget subprocess with a versioned
`audio-hooks.webhook.v1` payload, so they never block the hook.
The status bar offers 29 configurable segments, including
color-coded context-window and usage bars with absolute token
counts (e.g. `Context: 83% (166K/200K)`) so users see the
"agent dumb zone" before they reach it. The deepest piece is the
cross-editor architecture: canonical sources live in `/hooks/`,
`/bin/`, `/audio/`, `/cursor-hooks/`, and `/codex-hooks/`, and
`build-plugin.sh` syncs them into the Claude Code plugin layout
— one source of truth, three editor targets — with continuous
integration enforcing no-drift via `build-plugin.sh --check`.
Cursor rides its 3.2.16+ auto-bridge so most users get support
for free; Cursor-only installs use a native
`~/.cursor/hooks.json` registration guarded by a
`DUPLICATE_BRIDGE` error that aborts if both paths would run at
once. The Codex path handles OpenAI's required
`[features]\ncodex_hooks = true` flag by writing a fresh
`~/.codex/config.toml` when none exists and emitting
machine-readable `next_steps` for the agent to edit an existing
one — echook never round-trips a user's TOML, because that would
destroy comments and formatting. Canonical events with no Codex
equivalent no-op cleanly under the Codex invoker with a debug
log line, never blocking the session. The whole contract is
validated by 248 test cases across Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS on
Python 3.9, 3.12, and 3.13 in GitHub Actions.

**Outcomes:** ~76 GitHub stars (external — needs registry check; live 75),
151 commits across the 5.x → 6.x line (now v6.3.4), and 248
automated test cases. The defining release sequence: v5.0 (Jan
2026) shipped the AI-first redesign — JSON command interface,
`/audio-hooks` skill, NDJSON logging, plugin-native install;
v5.2.0 (May 2026) added native Codex CLI support with +33
contract tests; v5.2.1 (May 2026) executed the echook rebrand —
a "door-only" GitHub rename from `claude-code-audio-hooks` to
`echook` (leading with "Claude Code" had become misleading once
native Cursor and Codex paths shipped) that preserved every
existing install via GitHub's repo redirect and kept the
`audio-hooks` command name, the `chanmeng-audio-hooks`
marketplace name, and all state directories unchanged. The
Engram Architect Cohort Engagement Letter (May 9, 2026)
explicitly names *claude-code-audio-hooks* among Chan's
preserved IP — formal recognition by a commercial
agentic-infrastructure firm that this is portfolio-grade
engineering, not a hobby script. The sister repo
echook-promo-video then closed the loop on the thesis: an
88-second 1080p product film composed entirely in code (React +
Remotion, with Suno-generated background music and ElevenLabs
voice and effects — no video-editing suites and no human voice
actors), fully reproducible from two commands.

**My role:** Sole author of both repos. On echook proper: designed the
AI-first contract (JSON interface + NDJSON log + fixed error
codes + `suggested_command` recovery), wrote every hook-event
integration, engineered the three-editor synchronization
pipeline (canonical sources synced into the plugin layout via
`build-plugin.sh`, with CI enforcement), authored the Codex
TOML feature-flag bootstrap pattern, and executed the 5.2.1
rebrand without breaking compatibility. On echook-promo-video:
designed the nine-section narrative arc, built the reusable
visual primitives (TypewriterText, Terminal, SoundWave,
GreenGrid, CounterNumber, GlowText), orchestrated the Remotion
transitions, wrote the ElevenLabs audio-generation script, and
tuned the volume-ducking so the background music dips under
voiceover and demo playback. Maintains both repos today.
### ArchLang — An original declarative floor-plan language that compiles .arch source into professional, dimensioned floor plans — 'Typst/LaTeX for architecture.' Zero-dependency, isomorphic TypeScript (hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser), errors returned as data not thrown, with architectural-soundness linting, human-circulation analysis, a deterministic repair pass, five export formats (SVG/DXF/PDF/PNG/text), an agent-native CLI, full LSP + VS Code extension, and a client-side playground. Published to npm; the compiler engine behind ArchCanvas.
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://playground.archlang.uk
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/archlang
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript 5.7 (ESM, zero runtime dependencies), Hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser + expression evaluator, tsup (build), Biome 2.5 (lint/format), Vitest 2.1 + fast-check (property tests) + pixelmatch (visual regression), Optional lazy add-ons: @resvg/resvg-js (PNG), clipper2-wasm (geometry), pdfkit (PDF), LSP (hover/completion/rename/signature), VS Code extension (ChanMeng.archlang, TextMate grammar), Vite + CodeMirror 6 playground (100% client-side), VitePress docs, GitHub Action (renders ```arch fences to images)

**Summary:** ArchLang is a small language for floor plans: you describe walls, rooms, doors, windows, and furniture in plain text, and it compiles to a precise, dimensioned drawing — SVG, DXF, PDF, PNG, or a text-only plan. The same source always produces the identical drawing, so widening a bedroom is a one-number edit instead of re-drawing. It also checks whether a plan is actually livable (can you reach the bathroom? does a bedroom have a window?), and is the floor-plan engine behind the AI design agent ArchCanvas.

**Impact:** Describe a floor plan in plain text — walls, rooms, doors, windows — and get a precise, dimensioned architectural drawing back. Change one number and exactly one thing moves. It also checks that a plan is actually livable, and powers ArchCanvas, an AI design agent.

**Problem:** Floor plans live in two unsatisfying states: hand-drawn in heavy CAD tools, or generated as raster images. Both make the thing you most want to do — a precise, parametric edit — expensive: widening a bedroom means re-drawing, or re-rolling an image and hoping the rest survives. Neither is authorable by an AI agent, because there is no deterministic text representation an agent can write, verify, and tweak. And an image never *knows* whether the plan it depicts is actually habitable — whether a bathroom is reachable only through a bedroom, whether a door swings into another door, whether a bedroom has a window. ArchLang exists to make a floor plan a **program**: exact, deterministic, diff-able, agent-authorable, and checkable for architectural soundness.

**Audience:** Three audiences. Architects and self-builders who want plans that are precise and reproducible rather than hand-drawn approximations; design-tool builders who need a floor-plan engine they can embed (ArchCanvas is the first); and AI agents that need to author and verify plans without ever rendering an image — for which ArchLang exposes its entire language in one ~2k-token spec page, returns a plan "as facts" (rooms, areas, adjacency, door connections, circulation) over `describe --json`, and scores a plan against a written brief with `validate --intent`.

**Product logic:** You write `.arch` source. The coordinate model is integer millimetres with an SVG-convention origin; elements are placed either absolutely (`room 4000x3000 at (0,0)`) or **relationally** — `right-of`, `left-of`, `above`, `below` with `align` and `gap`, resolved by deterministic topological arithmetic so the layout stays consistent under edits. The language is genuinely programmable: `let` bindings, pure value-functions, `for`/`if`/`while`, arrays, string interpolation, optional metric unit suffixes (`4m`/`40cm`), and `import` + `component` for reuse — all expand-time and pure (no runtime, clock, or I/O), so output is byte-for-byte deterministic.

Compilation is a returned value, not a side effect: `compile()` hands back `{ svg, errors, warnings, diagnostics, ast, scene }` and **never throws** on a bad plan — every diagnostic carries a byte span and a machine-applicable `fix`, and the SVG is simply left empty. On top of the compiler sit several analysis surfaces: a **lint** pass over eight architectural-soundness rules (reachability, door-swing collision, windowless bedroom, unenclosed wet room, too-narrow door…); a **circulation** analysis that models how a person actually walks the plan on a clearance-eroded nav grid (per-room walk distance, narrowest pinch, path circuitousness) with an optional route overlay; a deterministic **repair** pass that nudges furniture out of walls and door swings and emits a change log; an **intent** check that scores the plan against a written brief and returns a pass/fail exit code; and a **suggest** surface that emits candidate door/window statements to fix reachability problems. All of it is reachable from a ~20-subcommand CLI where every command supports `--json` and returns deterministic exit codes, plus an LSP, a VS Code extension, four render themes, accessibility annotations, and a fully client-side playground.

**Business logic:** ArchLang is free and open-source, MIT, published on npm as `@chanmeng666/archlang`. Its strategic role is twofold. As an artifact it is a credential — an original programming language with a hand-written compiler, an 83-code diagnostic system, domain-specific static analysis, and full IDE tooling, built and versioned with engineering discipline (15 ADRs, 34 tagged releases). As infrastructure it is the **engine that makes a commercial product possible**: ArchCanvas (the AI design agent) depends on it, writing parametric ArchLang rather than freehand SVG so that its select-to-act editing, parametric sliders, and semantic diffs all work — capabilities that only exist because the underlying plan is a deterministic program. Agent access is designed CLI-first: a CLI costs zero context until it is called, whereas an MCP tool schema sits in an agent's window permanently. ArchLang still publishes an optional MCP server (`@chanmeng666/archlang-mcp`, on the official registry) so MCP-native hosts can discover it, but keeps the SDK quarantined in that one package so the core stays zero-dependency.

**Technical approach:** A pure-TypeScript, zero-runtime-dependency compiler: a hand-written lexer (line/col tracking), a recursive-descent parser that recovers rather than throwing, and a separate expression evaluator, all isomorphic so the same code runs in Node and in the browser — the playground sends nothing to a server. The heavy or native pieces (resvg for PNG raster, clipper2-wasm for polygon geometry, pdfkit for PDF) are **optional and lazily imported** behind bundler-ignore guards, so a browser bundle never tries to resolve them and a default install pulls none of them. Output determinism is enforced with property tests (fast-check) and visual-regression tests (pixelmatch). The diagnostic layer — 83 stable `E_*`/`W_*` codes, each with a byte span and a suggested fix, surfaced as data and even as a self-describing error-SVG card — is the deliberate foundation of the agent story: an LLM can author a plan, read the structured errors, and self-correct in a tight loop without a human or an image in the middle.

**Outcomes:** A complete, published language: 304 commits (297 mine, the rest automated dependency bumps) over roughly three weeks of intensive development (from 2026-06-25), across 92 source files (~23.3k LOC) against 109 test files, released to npm at v1.18.0 over 34 tagged releases with 15 architecture decision records. The surface area is unusually complete for a solo language project — 8 primitives, 5 export formats, 8 lint-rule modules, a circulation analyzer, a repair pass, an intent-scoring channel, a ~20-command CLI, a full LSP, a VS Code extension, an optional MCP server, a client-side playground, a docs site, a published HuggingFace dataset of self-verifying repair trajectories, and a GitHub Action that renders every ```arch fence in a repo's Markdown. Most tellingly, it is already load-bearing: ArchCanvas's in-browser use of the core drove real hardening, proving the engine under a downstream product rather than in isolation.

**My role:** I invented the language and wrote everything — the language design, the hand-written lexer / parser / evaluator, the 83-code diagnostic system, all five export backends, the lint / circulation / repair / intent / suggest pipelines, the LSP and VS Code extension, the CodeMirror playground, the docs site, the MCP server, and the npm packaging. 297 of the 304 commits are mine; the remaining 7 are automated dependency bumps.
### Vitex — AI Career Agent (vitex.org.nz) — Agent-ready AI career agent (vitex.org.nz) — turns a job description + free-text background into a tailored, ATS-optimized resume PDF + cover letter in ~30s via an 8-step pipeline. The whole product is reachable over an authenticated public HTTP API, plus a vitex-cli and a stdio MCP server (Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor); a DB partial-unique index makes each generation idempotent, so an SSE reconnect or a concurrent retry never re-runs the pipeline. Vercel AI SDK + gpt-5.5 (reasoning) / gpt-5.4-mini (extract) + Typst local compile (<100ms), on a DigitalOcean VPS via Docker + Traefik + GitHub Actions
[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://www.vitex.org.nz/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/easy-resume
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript 5, Vercel AI SDK v6, @ai-sdk/openai, OpenAI gpt-5.5 (reasoning) / gpt-5.4-mini (extract), Zod schemas, Typst (local binary), Neon Postgres, Drizzle ORM, Upstash Redis, Stack Auth, Stripe, Cloudinary, shadcn/ui + Phantom design system, Framer Motion, Public v1 HTTP API (OpenAPI spec + curl playbook in skill.md), vitex-cli (thin API client, bin `vitex`), MCP server (`vitex mcp` via npx, stdio — Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor), Docker, Traefik, DigitalOcean VPS, GitHub Actions CI/CD

**Summary:** AI career agent live at **vitex.org.nz**. Paste a job ad and describe your background in plain text, and an 8-step AI pipeline writes a resume and cover letter tailored to that specific job, then compiles them into a polished, ATS-ready PDF in about 30 seconds across 7 auto-selected templates — no document is rendered by an outside service. Vitex is **agent-ready**: everything the website does is also reachable over a public API, a command-line tool, and an MCP server, so a terminal or an AI assistant (Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT) can drive the whole product without a browser.

**Impact:** Paste a job ad and describe your background — it writes a resume and cover letter tailored to that job and builds them into a polished PDF in about 30 seconds. Works from a browser, the command line, or inside an AI assistant.

**Problem:** Most AI resume tools fail in one of two ways: (a) a thin chatbot wrapper that hands you a paragraph and calls it a resume — no PDF, no ATS-aware structure, no template intelligence; or (b) a form-filling site that spends a minute-plus rendering the same generic template every other user gets. Both miss the real job: the resume has to be tailored to the *specific* job ad, the document has to survive automated applicant-tracking (ATS) parsing, the visual template has to fit the target industry, and the whole round-trip has to feel instant. None of that is true of "paste your CV into ChatGPT."

**Audience:** Three groups. **Job seekers in live applications** — especially in the NZ / Australia / Asia-Pacific markets where Chan personally needed the tool and where vitex.org.nz deliberately lives. **Career-changers** who have to re-tailor every application — the people for whom rewriting a resume per job ad is the actual bottleneck. And **AI-engineering reviewers** assessing Chan's ability to ship a production multi-step AI agent with real users, real billing, and real operations — not a demo.

**Product logic:** The flow is deliberately short. (1) **Homepage** — paste a job ad and describe your background in free text; no CV upload, no field-by-field form. (2) **Generate** — click run and the 8-step pipeline streams its progress live (reading the job ad → reading your background → matching → tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter → building the PDF), so you watch each stage finish instead of staring at a spinner, then land in the **Editor** with a PDF preview, an ATS score with concrete feedback, and the matching cover letter. (3) **Refine & export** — a plain-English box ("make the leadership section stronger", "shorten the intro") re-does just that slice rather than regenerating everything; then download the PDF or publish a share link. A dashboard keeps a searchable history for signed-in users.

**Business logic:** Each run is a multi-call AI pipeline plus a document compile, so capacity is metered per successful result rather than left open-ended: the ledger records a run **exactly once, and only after a PDF actually compiles**, which makes a failed or retried attempt a non-event. Public **share links** give the product organic reach — a candidate who lands a job can credit Vitex, and the shared resume is itself a live demo of what the product produces. The full stack (auth, database, cache, payments, AI, PDF rendering) is shaped for a real user base, not a showcase.

**Technical approach:** **AI agent layer.** The pipeline runs on the **Vercel AI SDK** with the OpenAI provider across **eight sequential stages**, on two-tier model routing — a cheaper **extract** model (default `gpt-5.4-mini`) for parsing and a stronger **reasoning** model (default `gpt-5.5`) for tailoring, both overridable per deployment, with per-tier reasoning-effort control. Each stage emits a **Zod-validated** structured output:
1. **JD parsing** — role, seniority, must-have and nice-to-have skills, company signals
2. **Background parsing** — free-text description → structured skills/experience
3. **Match analysis** — candidate↔job fit profile
4. **Tailoring** — reorder, rewrite, and emphasise history toward the parsed job ad
5. **ATS scoring** — a **deterministic, no-LLM** check of parse-ability, with actionable feedback
6. **Cover letter** — generated from the same parsed pair
7. **Render** — auto-select a template + seeded design tokens, emit Typst source
8. **Compile** — Typst → PDF server-side; the PDF is the deliverable, so the run is committed only here

Right after tailoring, a deterministic **faithfulness gate** re-checks for fabricated skills or employers and triggers one bounded corrective re-tailor — an anti-hallucination guard with no model in the check itself. Every stage streams progress over **Server-Sent Events**. Inputs are read from `sessionStorage` rather than URL params so deep links can't leak a user's data.

**Document engine — Typst, not LaTeX.** PDFs are compiled by a **local Typst binary** inside the Docker image, with no call to any outside service; the project migrated from LaTeX to Typst on 2026-04-06 (same day as the Cloudflare Workers → DigitalOcean VPS move) because Typst compiles in milliseconds where LaTeX needs seconds and Workers couldn't run the binary at all. The image pre-warms the Typst package cache (FontAwesome) so cold starts don't pay a fetch cost. **7 templates** (academic, banking, compact, creative, executive, modern-cv, two-column) are auto-selected from parsed industry/role signals.

**Persistence + product stack.** Neon serverless Postgres + Drizzle ORM (resumes, history, credit ledger); Upstash Redis for short-lived cache; Stack Auth for sign-in and agent API keys; Stripe for the credit ledger; Cloudinary for uploads and rendered PDFs; nanoid for share tokens.

**UI — Phantom.** Next.js 15 App Router + React 19 + TypeScript. The **Phantom** design system — a soft, flat, single-hue aubergine-and-lavender look with light-weight type and generous whitespace — is the deliberate visual identity, and replaced an earlier Neobrutalism (thick borders, hard shadows) treatment in a 2026 UI pass. shadcn/ui + Tailwind for primitives, Framer Motion for transitions, react-hook-form + Zod for validation.

**Deployment + hardening.** Production runs as a single **Docker container** on a **DigitalOcean VPS** behind **Traefik** (reverse proxy + automatic TLS), shipped by **GitHub Actions** with **health-gated rolling deploys** and automatic database migrations. Recent hardening added site-wide security headers (Content-Security-Policy + HSTS in production), request rate-limiting on the API-key/credit/billing routes, and auth + rate-limiting + sandboxing on the PDF-compile endpoint. Two earlier platform migrations (Railway → CF Workers → VPS) and the LaTeX → Typst swap all shipped without an outage.

**Outcomes:** Live and operational at **vitex.org.nz**, serving real users end-to-end from a pasted job ad to a compiled PDF. **379 commits over ~18 months** (2025-01-11 → 2026-07-14), essentially sole-authored. Two platform migrations and a LaTeX → Typst engine swap shipped without a service outage, each step captured in the commit log (the `xz-utils`/`curl` Dockerfile fixes, FontAwesome package pre-cache, the OpenAI strict-JSON-schema regression, the `sessionStorage` move for sensitive input). A 2026 UI pass replaced Neobrutalism with the Phantom design system; a subsequent security/ops wave added production CSP + HSTS, rate limits on the billing and API-key routes, a sandboxed compile endpoint, health-gated rolling deploys, and a production runbook. Backed by **49 test files / 437 tests**.

**My role:** Sole engineer, sole designer, sole product owner. Designed the 8-step agent pipeline with Zod-validated stages (migrating the editor from CopilotKit to the Vercel AI SDK agent chat along the way), wrote every API route, built the Stripe credit system, set up Stack Auth, designed the Drizzle schema for resumes / credits / share tokens, ran both platform migrations (Railway → CF Workers → DigitalOcean VPS), executed the LaTeX → Typst engine swap (including the Dockerfile fixes and the FontAwesome package pre-cache), designed the Phantom UI that replaced the earlier Neobrutalism treatment, built the plain-English refinement loop in the Editor, upgraded the model routing to gpt-5.5 / gpt-5.4-mini with per-tier reasoning-effort control, and hardened production (CSP + HSTS, rate limits, sandboxed compile, health-gated rolling deploys). I continue to operate the live service.
### server-google-jobs — Companion early-MCP Google Jobs server (Dec 31 2024, 36 days post-MCP-launch) — subject of dedicated Skywork AI deep-dive review, 4-language localized, listed across 10+ MCP catalogs
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/server-google-jobs
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript, Node.js, @modelcontextprotocol/sdk 1.25.1, stdio transport, SerpAPI, node-fetch, ES modules, npm (scoped @chanmeng666), Dockerfile, .mcpb compiled binary, mcp-evals (v2.0.0 early adoption)

**Summary:** A Model Context Protocol (MCP) server — the open standard that
lets AI assistants call outside tools — that searches Google job
listings through SerpAPI and returns results in English,
Simplified Chinese, Japanese, or Korean. Built on Dec 31 2024,
just 36 days after MCP's public launch, as a companion to the
Google News MCP server from the same author. Installs from npm,
Smithery, Docker, or a one-file `.mcpb` bundle, and is listed
across MCP discovery catalogs including Glama, PulseMCP, mcp.so,
and Smithery (catalog ratings and visitor counts are external
claims — needs registry check).

**Impact:** The companion to the Google News server: lets AI assistants search Google job listings in English, Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. Built 36 days after MCP's launch — early enough that the founders of two major MCP catalogs contributed code to it.

**Problem:** When it was built at the very end of December 2024, the Model
Context Protocol was only weeks old and there was no ready-made
way for an AI assistant to look up job listings — least of all
in more than one language. A job seeker in Greater China, Japan,
or Korea who wanted Claude Desktop to find relevant openings had
to translate their own query and hand-assemble search-URL
parameters.

**Audience:** Three groups: (a) developers wiring job search into an AI
application; (b) multilingual job seekers using Claude Desktop
or another MCP-aware assistant; and (c) other early MCP authors
looking for a worked example of locale-aware integration with a
third-party API. (Skywork AI's write-up describes it as a
reference pattern from the early MCP ecosystem — external claim,
needs registry check.)

**Product logic:** The server exposes Google Jobs (through SerpAPI) as a single MCP
tool, `search_jobs`. A caller passes a `query` plus optional
filters — `location`, `radius`, `employment_type`, `salary`,
`posted_age`, `sort_by`, and `page` — and picks the output
language with `hl` (`en`, `zh-CN`, `ja`, or `ko`). It returns up
to 10 results per page, with every label, status message, and
suggestion translated into the chosen language (24 translated
strings per locale) so the assistant can present results
directly without extra formatting.

**Technical approach:** TypeScript on Node.js, built against the official
`@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` (kept current at 1.25.1). Rather
than branch on language throughout the code, the four locales
live in one translation table keyed to shared field names, so
the same query logic serves every language and only the output
strings swap. Distribution is deliberately wide for an early MCP
server: npm (`@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server`), one-command
Smithery install, a Dockerfile for containers, and a
self-contained `.mcpb` bundle. `package.json` also carries a
structured `ai:` block (use cases, capabilities, target
audience) so LLM-driven catalogs can index it — the same
discovery-oriented authoring pattern across this portfolio's MCP
work.

**Outcomes:** 75 commits on the main branch, about 48 by Chan and the rest
split between Renovate dependency updates and outside
contributors — notably the founders of the Smithery (Henry Mao)
and Glama (Frank Fiegel) MCP catalogs, a real early-ecosystem
signal. Carries security-assessment badges from MseeP.ai and
Glama.ai in the README. Listed across MCP discovery catalogs —
Glama, PulseMCP, mcp.so, Smithery, and others (10+ surfaces,
A-rating, PulseMCP visitor count, and the Skywork AI deep-dive
review are external claims — need registry/catalog check).
Shipped one day after its companion Google News MCP server,
making the pair one of the earliest public sets of locale-aware
MCP servers.

**My role:** Sole author. I designed the single-table localization approach,
wrote every translation, built the multi-channel distribution
(npm, Smithery, Docker, `.mcpb`), and maintain it alongside its
Google News MCP counterpart. External contributions have been
dependency bumps and catalog-integration commits; the tool
design and localization are mine.
### AI Programming Education Platform (programming.chanmeng.org) — Bilingual Docusaurus teaching platform — three years of AI/programming cohorts running (five cohort versions side-by-side, three of them concurrent in 2026) with an in-course RAG AI assistant on Cloudflare Workers (Llama 3.1 8B + Vectorize + KV), Notion-backed Capstone voting, and Typst-generated PDF handouts; the active home for the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the clearest evidence of Chan as an AI educator/evangelist, not only a builder
[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://programming.chanmeng.org/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/ai-programming-teaching-project
- **Tech stack:** Docusaurus 3.8.1, @docusaurus/faster, React 18, MDX, Cloudflare Workers, Workers AI (Llama 3.1 8B Instruct (fp8)), Cloudflare Vectorize, BGE Base EN v1.5 (768-dim embeddings), Cloudflare KV, Cloudflare Pages, Notion API, Algolia DocSearch (contextual), Typst, Mermaid (dual-view), i18next (EN + 简体中文), Server-Sent Events

**Summary:** Production teaching platform at programming.chanmeng.org where beginners learn to build real software with AI tools, taught across three years of cohorts. Five course versions stay online side by side — from the 2024 class to three separate 2026 tracks (TechNest, Peyvand Academy, Her Waka) — each with video lessons, lab guides, and downloadable Typst-generated PDF notes. A built-in AI tutor (a RAG assistant on Cloudflare Workers AI, Llama 3.1 8B) answers questions from a student's own cohort material, and a Demo Day page publishes the projects students shipped. Bilingual EN + 简体中文. 211 of 220 non-merge commits solo (~96%), actively maintained through the 2026 TechNest cohort.

**Impact:** A public website that teaches beginners to build software with AI — three years of cohorts kept side by side (five course versions, three running in 2026), plus an in-site AI tutor that answers from each class's own lessons and a Demo Day page for students' finished projects.

**Problem:** AI-programming cohort programmes typically die between cohorts: the lecture deck is rebuilt from scratch each round, students from the previous cohort lose access to their materials, and the "AI tutor" experience — when it exists at all — is a chatbot tab the student opens separately, with no idea which cohort's curriculum they're studying. Meanwhile the instructor has no way to keep older cohorts permanently retrievable without disturbing the live one. The result: every cohort feels like a one-shot, course content compounds nowhere, and the AI-assisted-development experience is itself unassisted by AI.

**Audience:** Three concentric layers. Active cohort students — the 2026 TechNest AI Track, plus the 2026 Peyvand Academy and academyEX Her Waka programmes — who watch video recordings, follow lab guides, download PDF handouts, and ask the in-site AI tutor version-aware questions. Past cohort alumni (2024 Winter, 2025 Summer) who keep stable URLs to their original materials. Public learners and prospective employers who reach programming.chanmeng.org from the profile and use it as proof of production-grade EdTech built with native AI, not just slides.

**Product logic:** Visitors land on programming.chanmeng.org and the docs default to the 2026 TechNest version (the active cohort), while the version switcher exposes 2026 Her Waka, 2026 Peyvand Academy, 2025 Summer, and 2024 Winter side by side — an alum of any cohort hits a stable permalink. Each version ships its own Video Tutorials section, weekly lab guides as both web docs and downloadable Typst-generated PDFs, and a Mermaid dual-view diagram (the same diagram in two presentations so visual and textual learners both succeed). A floating AI Chat Assistant answers in natural language by retrieving the current cohort's content first (version-aware retrieval), then falling back to other cohorts and the blog. During Demo Day the TechNest cohort gets a public Capstone Showcase where invited guests view each team's finished project and vote through a Notion-backed flow.

**Business logic:** Theory of change: one teaching platform that compounds cohort over cohort instead of resetting each round. Once the 2024 Winter materials are versioned in, they stay at the same URL forever; the next cohort's materials sit alongside them without conflict. The AI tutor means a student writing code at midnight gets context-aware help against their own cohort's syllabus, not generic chatbot answers. For Chan personally: the platform is production-grade EdTech engineering (Cloudflare Workers AI + Vectorize + versioned bilingual docs + a PDF pipeline) built while doing actual teaching work — every commit is both a portfolio artefact and student value.

**Technical approach:** Built on Docusaurus 3.8.1 with the experimental @docusaurus/faster build pipeline. Five cohort versions coexist through Docusaurus versioned docs — each version is a self-contained doc tree with its own sidebar, blog feed, and tags. Bilingual i18n is end-to-end: EN primary, 简体中文 secondary, with Algolia DocSearch set to contextual locale + version filtering so a Mandarin-speaking 2026 TechNest student gets 2026 TechNest results in Chinese by default.

RAG AI assistant on Cloudflare Workers AI. A separate Cloudflare Worker (worker/src/index.ts) handles POST /api/chat behind the custom domain programming-api.chanmeng.org:
- the query is embedded with BGE Base EN v1.5 (768-dim) and sent to Cloudflare Vectorize, which returns the top chunks from the indexed corpus (~4,800 chunks spanning all five cohort versions + the blog, keyed by stable content-addressed chunk IDs so re-indexing is idempotent)
- retrieval is version-aware: a 2026-technest student gets 2026-technest results by default, with older cohorts available on explicit ask
- the context is passed to Workers AI Llama 3.1 8B Instruct (fp8) which generates the answer, streamed back to the in-site ChatWidget over Server-Sent Events; per-session history persists in Cloudflare KV
- the chat endpoint is rate-limited (per-IP, plus a Cloudflare WAF burst rule) with an explicit CORS allow-list

Typst PDF handout pipeline. The same Typst skill taught in the curriculum is the build tool that produces the printable curriculum outline, capstone spec, and per-week lab guides — students see in production the artefact they're taught to build. A Mermaid dual-view component renders every diagram twice (rendered + source) so visual and textual learners both succeed.

Capstone classroom infrastructure. A Cloudflare Worker proxies writes into Notion so cohort owners moderate inside the tool they already use; the Capstone 2026 public showcase lists each team's demo with live Notion-backed voting open to invited guests. Deployed end-to-end on Cloudflare's edge: Pages for the static Docusaurus build, Workers for the chat + capstone backend, Vectorize for the vector index, KV for sessions and votes.

**Outcomes:** Live at programming.chanmeng.org, in active use as the official platform for the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the 2026 academyEX Her Waka programme. 211 of 220 non-merge commits (95.9%) solo across Chan's two GitHub identities; the remainder is AI auto-review + Dependabot. Five cohort versions run side by side without interfering. The RAG index covers all five versions + the blog (~4,800 chunks), with version-aware retrieval and stable chunk IDs. After Cloudflare deprecated the original chat model, the assistant was restored on 2026-07-10 by swapping to Llama 3.1 8B Instruct (fp8), alongside a move to a custom API domain, per-IP rate limiting, a CORS allow-list, and a WAF rule. Course recordings continue through the TechNest cohort's 8-week curriculum, and Demo Day 2026 now has a public Capstone Showcase with published student projects (CreditHero, iCare, JobOrg-AI) and guest voting. DeepWiki provides an external AI doc-Q&A surface at deepwiki.com/ChanMeng666/ai-programming-teaching-project.

**My role:** I built and run the entire platform end-to-end: Docusaurus configuration, the versioned-docs taxonomy across five cohorts, every cohort's curriculum content, the Cloudflare Worker RAG backend (worker entry, chat handler, RAG module, Vectorize seeding, KV session store), the BGE-Base embeddings ingestion pipeline, the Typst PDF handout build, the Mermaid dual-view component, the Notion-Worker proxy and Demo Day showcase for Capstone voting, the Algolia contextual-search configuration, and the bilingual i18n across every surface. I migrated production from Vercel to Cloudflare Pages in 2026-04, and in 2026-07 hardened the chat backend (custom domain, rate limiting, CORS allow-list, WAF) and restored it onto Cloudflare's current model after the old one was deprecated. I continue to ship the current cohort's recordings and lab content each week.
### Aotearoa AI Hackathon 2026 — Event Q&A AI Assistant (She Sharp) — Industry-partnered AI event assistant for the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival — relaunched for 2026 (AUT City Campus) as a single-page voice-first agent: an audio-reactive Three.js orb driving a two-way OpenAI Realtime voice conversation (WebRTC + ephemeral keys) plus gpt-4o-mini text chat, over a Neon knowledge base + transcripts with KV rate-limiting, on Vercel. The 2025 edition (AUT × AI Forum NZ × She Sharp) shipped a 3D sprite mascot with 6-tier affection gamification + a Notion-as-database FAQ on Cloudflare Workers; generalized into an open-source MIT template
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://hackathon.shesharp.org.nz/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/event-qa-ai-template
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 16.1 (App Router), React 18 + TypeScript 5, Tailwind CSS v3 + Framer Motion v12, Vercel AI SDK v4.3, @ai-sdk/openai (GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18 streaming), Three.js 0.182 + @react-three/fiber 8.16, Custom Quantum Orb 3D mascot (CSS 3D transforms + Three.js particles), Sprite affection gamification engine (gesture detection, mood weighting, time-of-day bias), Notion REST API (raw fetch, CF Workers compatible — replaced @notionhq/client SDK), shadcn/ui + Radix primitives, @opennextjs/cloudflare adapter, Cloudflare Workers deployment (migrated from Vercel), wrangler v4 CI/CD, localStorage state persistence (50-msg chat history + sprite affection state), 5-min FAQ client cache + optimistic vote/view updates, AI rate limiting + per-session token budgets + prompt guardrails (2026-07 security hardening), OpenAI Realtime API over WebRTC — two-way voice (2026 relaunch), Ephemeral client secrets minted server-side (OPENAI_API_KEY never reaches the browser), Audio-reactive Three.js orb voice UI + live captions (2026), Vercel deployment (2026 edition — she-sharp1/aihackathon-2026)
- **Part of:** She Sharp (https://shesharp.org.nz)
- **Related projects:** She Sharp

**Summary:** Real industry-partnered AI event assistant built for the **AI
Hackathon Festival 2025** (Auckland, Aug 15–16, 2025), co-organised
by **Auckland University of Technology**, **AI Forum New Zealand**,
and **She Sharp**, with **UN Sustainable Development Goals** as
the innovation brief. The platform serves participants and mentors
through three interlocking systems: (1) an **interactive 3D sprite
mascot** with a 6-tier "Stranger → Soulmate" affection system,
7-state dynamic mood engine, 17 emotion categories, gesture
recognition (circle/pat/swipe), negative-behaviour penalties,
streak bonuses, and time-of-day emotion biases — a fully gamified
personality layer that responds to how users actually touch the
page; (2) a **Notion-as-database community FAQ** with up/downvote
ranking, view tracking, 5-minute client cache, and graceful static
fallback when Notion is unreachable; (3) an **OpenAI GPT-4o-mini
streaming chat** with markdown-rendered responses and one-click
FAQ-to-chat hand-off. After the live deployment, the codebase was
generalised into a reusable open-source template
(`event-qa-ai-template` on GitHub, MIT) so any future hackathon /
conference can fork-and-config the same stack. Chan was
subsequently recognised among the **14 expert mentors** at the
festival.

For the **2026 festival** (Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival 2026,
AUT City Campus) the project was **rebuilt from the ground up** as
a single-page **voice-first** agent: the whole site is a centered,
audio-reactive Three.js orb that starts a **two-way voice
conversation over the OpenAI Realtime API via WebRTC** (the client
exchanges SDP directly with OpenAI using a short-lived ephemeral
secret, so the real API key never reaches the browser), with a
text-input fallback answered by gpt-4o-mini via the Vercel AI SDK,
live captions, and a collapsible event-info panel. Agent
instructions build from a Neon `knowledge` table (graceful
fallback to config), transcripts persist best-effort, and Upstash
KV rate-limits token minting. Deployed on Vercel
(`she-sharp1/aihackathon-2026`, live at aihackathon-2026.vercel.app).

**Impact:** Voice-first AI event agent for the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival — relaunched 2026 (AUT) as an audio-reactive orb driving two-way OpenAI Realtime voice + gpt-4o-mini text chat; the 2025 edition served 11 teams / 80+ participants with a gamified 3D sprite mascot + Notion FAQ.

**Problem:** Hackathons and conferences generate the same Q&A surface every
year: participants ask the same 30 questions ("how do I form a
team", "what counts as AI", "when do I have to be on site",
"what's the prize structure", "where do I find a mentor"), and
organisers answer them via static PDFs that go stale the moment
the schedule shifts. Bolt-on chatbots exist but they treat
humans as passive consumers — a popup widget in the corner, a
knowledge base scraped at build time, no memory of what the
community itself finds helpful. Three structural gaps stack up:
(1) the FAQ is **authored once** rather than **curated by the
community**, so quality drifts; (2) the AI is a generic
wrapper around a chat API, with no event-specific personality
or theme tie-in; (3) organisers cannot update content without
a code redeploy, so the platform calcifies the moment the
original developer hands it off. The AI Hackathon Festival
2025 organisers needed a platform that solved all three on the
timeline of a real event with 80+ participants and three
co-organising institutions reviewing every word on screen.

**Audience:** Direct users were the **80+ participants and 14 expert mentors**
of AI Hackathon Festival 2025 — students from Auckland
universities, **Westlake Girls High School** secondary students,
and AI Forum NZ industry mentors all asking the same FAQ-style
questions across a 26-hour residential event. Secondary
audience: the **three co-organising institutions** (AUT, AI
Forum NZ, She Sharp) who use the platform as a public-facing
artefact of their joint programme. Tertiary audience: future
hackathon and conference organisers in NZ and abroad who can
fork the open-source template (`event-qa-ai-template`) and
deploy their own event Q&A AI in under an hour by editing four
configuration files — `site.config.ts`, `ai.config.ts`,
`content.config.ts`, `branding.config.ts` — without touching
component code.

**Product logic:** The product is structured as three layers that compose
independently. Layer one is the **3D welcoming surface**: the
landing page renders a particle sphere with a "WELCOME TO AI
HACKATHON!" greeting set against a `CosmicBackground` Three.js
scene, with `Calendar` / `MapPin` event-info widgets, sponsor
logos for the three co-organisers, and a glassmorphism Google
Maps modal showing AUT WG Building. Layer two is the **chat
page** — a 70/30 desktop split between a full-screen OpenAI
chat (with `react-markdown` + `rehype-highlight` for
code-block syntax highlighting) and a community FAQ sidebar.
Layer three — the differentiator — is the **interactive sprite
mascot** that lives alongside the chat: a CSS-3D **Quantum
Singularity Orb** (accretion disk + photon ring + event
horizon + energy jets, all built from layered divs with
`transform-style: preserve-3d` and `rotateX(60deg)`) that
becomes a fully gamified personality. The user can **circle**,
**swipe vertically/horizontally**, **pat**, or **click** the
orb; the gesture detection engine tracks each interaction and
feeds it through an affection/mood scoring system. Five
gestures grant affection (circle x5 = +10 affection / "Wheee~!
So dizzy but fun!"), five negative behaviours penalise it
(`roughMovement`, `spamClick`, `suddenLeave`, `longIgnore`,
`excessiveCircle`), and the resulting state drives which of
17 emotions the sprite expresses — including three **rare**
emotions (`devoted`, `starry`, `mischievous`) only unlocked
at the top affection tiers. The result: users do not just chat
with an AI, they **build a relationship with a tiny digital
creature** while doing so — a memorable, shareable layer that
turns a Q&A bot into a piece of event identity.

**Business logic:** Three intertwined value propositions. For the **organising
institutions**, the platform is a public-facing demonstration
artefact of the joint AUT × AI Forum NZ × She Sharp programme,
running on Cloudflare Workers (free tier covers an event-week
traffic spike with zero cost) with a Notion database that
**non-developer event staff can edit live** during the event —
adding new FAQ entries through the Notion UI, watching them
appear within the 5-minute client cache window. For **future
event organisers**, after the live event, the codebase was
deliberately generalised into a **reusable MIT template**
(`event-qa-ai-template`) with all event-specific content
extracted into `config/*.ts` files and full setup
documentation, so the same stack ships any hackathon /
conference / community event Q&A platform in under an hour of
configuration. For **Chan**, the project established a
verifiable, AI-Forum-NZ-endorsed track record of shipping
commercial-grade AI infrastructure for an industry partner —
a credential cited in the public LinkedIn announcement of the
14-mentor cohort and in the Engram Architect Cohort
Engagement Letter narrative.

**Technical approach:** **AI layer.** OpenAI GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18 served through
Vercel AI SDK v4.3's `streamText` with `convertToCoreMessages`,
returning `toDataStreamResponse()` for character-by-character
SSE streaming. The system prompt is **generated from
siteConfig at runtime** so any organiser fork inherits an
event-specific prompt without code edits — it embeds event
name, dates, venue, theme (UN SDGs), team-formation rules
(3-6 members, Hacker/Hipster/Hustler composition), schedule
(Friday 8 PM venue close, Discord for mentor requests), and
prize tiers ($250 venue, $1,000 national, $5,000 national
Agentic AI winner) plus Markdown formatting guidelines. The
codebase pivoted from Google Gemini 2.5 Flash to OpenAI
GPT-4o-mini mid-build (commit `5eb3e70`) for cost + Cloudflare
Workers compatibility reasons; the system prompt generator
remained provider-agnostic. **3D sprite gamification.** A
~960-line `sprite-affection.config.ts` declaratively encodes
the entire gameplay model: 5 gestures, 5 negative behaviours,
7 mood states (`ecstatic` 80–100 → `upset` -100 to -80),
17 emotions with per-emotion ear-colour RGB, glow-colour,
particle-colour, animation type (`bounce` / `shake` / `pulse`
/ `spin` / `wobble` / `tremble` / `droop`) and eye shape
(`sphere` / `arc` / `star` / `heart` / `crescent` / `spiral`
/ `teardrop`), 6 affection tiers (Stranger 0–9 → Acquaintance
10–29 → Friend 30–59 → Good Friend 60–99 → Best Friend
100–149 → Soulmate 150+), 7 time-of-day biases (late-night
boosts `sleepy` x3, evening boosts `love` x1.3), and 5 streak
bonus levels (1/3/7/14/30 days, 30-day streak unlocks the
`devoted` rare emotion). Two relational systems link mood
and affection: a **mood multiplier** scales affection gains
from 0.5x (depressed) to 1.5x (euphoric), and **affection
mood protection** sets a floor that rises with tier (Soulmate
gets a -10 floor + 5 mood/min recovery toward +20). All state
persists to `localStorage` under `sprite-affection-data`.
The Quantum Orb itself is rendered with **CSS-only 3D**
(preserve-3d transforms + rotateX) + Three.js particle
accents — no GLB / heavy 3D pipeline — so the bundle stays
small enough for Cloudflare Workers. **Notion backend.** A
non-obvious engineering call: the project initially used
`@notionhq/client`, but migrating to Cloudflare Workers
(commit `5b1dc7e`) broke the Node-dependent SDK; the fix
(commit `20eaf2e`) replaced it with **direct REST calls
against `api.notion.com/v1` using native `fetch`** — version
header `Notion-Version: 2022-06-28`, filter by
`IsActive` checkbox, parse `Title` / `RichText` / `Select` /
`Number` properties via small typed getters. Four API routes
— `/api/chat`, `/api/faq/questions`, `/api/faq/vote`,
`/api/faq/view` — wrap the same client. **Deployment.**
Migrated from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers using
`@opennextjs/cloudflare` v1.3 + wrangler v4; the entire stack
now ships on Cloudflare's free tier including the AI streaming
endpoint. The build command `opennextjs-cloudflare build &&
opennextjs-cloudflare deploy` handles the Next.js → Workers
adapter step.

**Outcomes:** Delivered live for the **AI Hackathon Festival 2025** at AUT
City Campus WG Building, **15–16 August 2025**. Used by **11
teams and 80+ participants** during the residential event —
AI Forum NZ's public LinkedIn cohort announcement (with full
Westlake Girls High School student attendance) cites Chan as
**1 of 14 expert mentors**, recognised at the event. The
teams shipped solutions across the UN SDG brief — food
insecurity, digital accessibility, workforce upskilling, and
indigenous environmental stewardship — with national-tier
prizes flowing through the AI Forum NZ pipeline (AI Summit
entry worth $1,499, $1,000 national winner, $5,000 national
Agentic AI winner, ArcGIS licences). Post-event, the codebase
was **refactored into a reusable MIT template** (commit
`948bdf0` "transform project into reusable event Q&A
template"), documented in `docs/TEMPLATE-SETUP.md`,
`docs/NOTION-INTEGRATION.md`, and `docs/UI-DESIGN-SYSTEM.md`,
with the live deployment retained as the canonical worked
example. Listed alongside Chan's other commercial-grade AI
platforms (echook, vitex, chinese-redefine,
ai-programming-teaching-project) as portfolio evidence of
end-to-end industry-partner delivery — design, AI integration,
gameplay engineering, database, deployment, and post-delivery
productisation as a reusable open-source artefact.

**My role:** Sole developer end-to-end. Owned the discovery phase with the
three co-organising institutions, designed the dual-layer
product (chat surface + gamified mascot + Notion FAQ), built
every component, authored both the Gemini and the OpenAI
provider implementations, designed the entire ~960-line
affection / mood / emotion gameplay schema, engineered the
CSS-3D Quantum Orb visualisation, migrated the deployment
from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers when free-tier economics
and Notion-SDK incompatibility forced the move, refactored
the codebase into a reusable open-source template after the
live event, and authored the three setup-documentation files.
On the mentorship side: served as one of the 14 expert
mentors during the 26-hour event itself, guiding teams on
prompt design, SDG framing, and product narrative —
simultaneously building the platform and using it.
### gradient-svg-generator (Chromaflow) — Streaming Animated SVG Service for GitHub READMEs — 355 parametric animated SVG templates served as live XML via Vercel serverless — paste a URL in any Markdown file and the browser animates the gradient locally using SVG <animate>/SMIL + CSS @keyframes + SVG filters (feTurbulence, feDisplacementMap, feGaussianBlur). 18-month / 283-commit codebase taken through a documented 4-phase refactor (extract primitives -> truth-up marketing numbers -> JS->TS migration with Zod validation -> virtualized template gallery). Internal product name "Chromaflow".
[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/gradient-svg-generator
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 16.2 (App Router + Vercel serverless runtime), React 19.2 + TypeScript 5.4 (gradual JS->TS migration through Phase 7), Tailwind CSS 4.2 + @tailwindcss/typography + @tailwindcss/forms, Zustand 5.0 (template gallery state), @tanstack/react-query 5.83 (data fetching), @tanstack/react-virtual 3.13 (virtualized template grid for 355 entries), Zod 3.23 (runtime validation of /api/svg query params, 400-on-bad-input), fuse.js 7.1 (fuzzy template search), framer-motion 12.23 (visual editor micro-interactions), SVG <animate> / <animateTransform> (SMIL animation primitives), CSS @keyframes embedded in <style> inside the streamed SVG, Animated linearGradient / radialGradient stop offsets, SVG filters: <feTurbulence>, <feDisplacementMap>, <feGaussianBlur>, <feColorMatrix>, <textPath> for text-following-path animations, @vercel/node 2.15 serverless function (/api/svg endpoint streams XML), Vitest 1.6 + @testing-library/* (tests/contract/ + tests/unit/), ESLint 9 + TypeScript-ESLint 8 + Prettier 3 + Husky 9 + lint-staged 15, web-vitals 5.0 (production performance telemetry), ISC license

**Summary:** A free tool that turns a single link into a live, animated banner
for GitHub profiles and README files — paste the URL into any
Markdown file and the banner animates on its own, with nothing to
install. It ships 355 ready-made designs across 19 themes (pride
flags, tech, nature, gaming, and more) and a point-and-click
editor for people who don't want to hand-write links. Open-source
and built solo over 18 months; it powers every badge on this
profile, so a broken render shows up here first.

**Impact:** Paste one URL into any GitHub README and a live animated banner appears, no install. 355 free, open-source banner designs power every badge in this profile.

**Problem:** Giving a GitHub profile, project README, or docs site a
striking animated header usually means one of three bad
options: hand-authoring SVG (a multi-day job per banner),
paying a third-party service that adds a watermark or a narrow
style range, or dropping in a static image that looks dated
within months. None of these scale to the modern norm of
*hundreds* of repos per developer, each wanting its own look.
The specific gap: no zero-install service lets you drop a
single link into Markdown and get a live animated banner the
browser renders on its own — no JavaScript, no video player —
that also survives GitHub's image proxy (which strips most
interactivity). The gap is widest for identity-coded
communities: pride creators wanting accurate flag palettes,
technical writers wanting one consistent capsule shape across
docs, niche aesthetics (cyberpunk, art deco, impressionist)
with no ready-made gallery.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. (1) Open-source maintainers and
developer-builders who want polished READMEs and profile pages
without pulling in extra dependencies — the primary daily
users. (2) Identity-coded communities with template subsets
authored for them: 20 pride-flag templates (Progress, Trans,
Bi, Pan, Lesbian, Nonbinary, Ace, Genderfluid, Aromantic,
Rainbow, and more), a large GitHub-Profile set oriented around
developer branding, and experimental/creative-technologist
designs. (3) Engineers studying the project as a worked example
of parametric SVG animation built without a heavy graphics
framework, a deliberately documented multi-phase refactor, and
the "truth up marketing numbers" discipline of correcting your
own inflated counts.

**Product logic:** Single-URL contract. The user pastes one Markdown image line:
`![banner](https://gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/api/svg?text=Hello&template=neural-network&height=120)`
and GitHub (or any Markdown renderer) embeds the live animated
SVG. Ten URL parameters control everything: `text=` (the
display string, `;`-separated for skill-pill grids), `template=`
(any of the 355 template names), `height=`, `duration=` (loop
time), `color0=`…`colorN=` (gradient-stop overrides, hex without
`#`), `gradientType=` (an effect-style override that bypasses
the template default), `stroke=`, `strokeWidth=`, `rotate=`,
`textBg=`. People who don't want to hand-write parameters open
the /create visual editor — a point-and-click composer with live
preview that emits the final URL to copy. People who want to
browse first hit /templates, a virtualized gallery
(`@tanstack/react-virtual`) with fuzzy search across names,
categories, and tags, so all 355 entries scroll smoothly on any
device.

**Business logic:** Free, ISC-licensed, no paywall, no API key, no watermark. The
whole service runs as a Vercel serverless function that streams
the SVG document straight back to the browser
(`Content-Type: image/svg+xml`), cached at Vercel's edge. The
build-in-public value: the project is shared infrastructure for
Chan's other work — every visual badge across every
Chan-authored README pulls from this one service, so the service
is its own continuous test signal (any rendering regression
breaks visible branding on her own portfolio, forcing a same-day
fix). It's also the project that took the most explicit honesty
audit: commits `fdcb7dd` "truth up marketing numbers",
`db724e0` "drop 50+ Effects claim", and `8fd7886` "catch older
stale counts" walked the codebase correcting every exaggerated
figure to the real template count — engineering discipline that
turns marketing claims into checkable numbers instead of letting
generated copy inflate them.

**Technical approach:** **The SVG-animation technique is the load-bearing engineering
here.** Every banner is a small live XML document — typically
2–8 KB — that the browser animates on its own with no
JavaScript runtime, using three orthogonal SVG mechanisms in
combination:

(1) **SMIL animation elements** (`<animate>`,
`<animateTransform>`, `<animateMotion>`) animate XML attributes
over time — most often animating a
`<linearGradient>`/`<radialGradient>`'s stop `offset` values so
colors flow (rainbow-wave, hologram-blue, northern-aurora), or
rotating a pattern (zigzag-energy, cyber-matrix). This is the
declarative channel: the whole timeline is XML, no scripting.

(2) **CSS `@keyframes` embedded in a `<style>` block inside the
SVG.** Where SMIL falls short (animating filter strength,
`stroke-dasharray` reveals), CSS keyframes take over —
typewriter-terminal, glitch-cyber, neon-flicker, luminance-glow,
chrome-flow. Same technique used in the sister project
github-readme-suno-cards: CSS keyframes survive GitHub's
image-proxy sanitization pass.

(3) **SVG filter primitives** for organic effects neither SMIL
nor CSS can produce — turbulent-waves, liquid-mercury,
plasma-blob, caustic-underwater all feed `<feTurbulence>`
(fractal noise) into `<feDisplacementMap>` for distortion, plus
`<feGaussianBlur>` and `<feColorMatrix>` for bloom/glow.
Path-text templates layer `<textPath>` on a moving `<path>` so
text follows a curve.

**Template organisation is the architectural bet.** 355
templates split across 19 named categories (Pride, Tech, Nature,
Material, Text-FX, Gaming, Weather, Light & Shadow, Art
Movements, Culinary, Patterns, Metallic, Path-Text, Shapes,
Experimental, GitHub-Profile, and more) plus base gradient
types. A documented refactor saga did the structural work:
Phase 5 extracted shared `svgPrimitives` factories and
`_shared/palettes.js` and moved to feature-folders; Phase 7 was
a JavaScript→TypeScript migration in seven sub-phases plus
Zod-validated /api/svg query params (a request like
`?height=99999999999` returns a clean typed error instead of
crashing the function); Phase 8 virtualized the gallery.
Deployment: Vercel serverless. Testing: Vitest with
`tests/contract/` guarding the URL-parameter contract across
refactors, plus `tests/unit/`. Quality gates: Husky pre-commit
`eslint --fix` + `prettier --write` via `lint-staged`;
`web-vitals` telemetry in production. The July 2026 "Ink Room ×
Living Color" redesign refreshed the whole site UI, added a
gradient footer wordmark, and gave the project its own OG
social-share card.

**Outcomes:** 283 commits across 18 months (first commit 2025-01-06, latest
2026-07-10), effectively solo — every commit is Chan's except
one automated GitHub Action commit. Production availability on
Vercel's global edge, ISC-licensed for broad reuse. Dogfooded
across every Chan-authored repo: the badges, banners, and
section headers in every public README (including the one that
ships this profile) come from
`gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/api/svg` URLs, so any
rendering regression breaks visible branding immediately and
forces a same-day fix — a continuous, self-testing loop. The
multi-phase refactor saga is itself an outcome worth surfacing:
Phase 5 (extract primitives, palettes, feature-folders), Phase 6
(truth up the marketing numbers; author `docs/architecture.md`
and an `adding-an-effect` walkthrough), Phase 7 (JS→TS in seven
sub-phases plus Zod validation), Phase 8 (gallery
virtualization), followed by the July 2026 "Ink Room × Living
Color" full-site redesign and a new OG share card. The honesty
discipline is rare enough in solo open source to flag on its
own: Chan went through the codebase and corrected exaggerated
counts rather than letting generated copy inflate them. Roadmap:
continued template additions and broader category coverage.

**My role:** Sole author. Designed the URL-parameterised streaming-SVG
contract; authored every one of the 355 template generators
across 19 categories; designed the parametric template system so
each template is a self-contained function rather than a
monolithic switch; engineered the SVG-animation toolkit
combining SMIL, CSS keyframes, and SVG filters; built the
`/create` visual editor with live preview and the `/templates`
virtualized gallery; ran the full multi-phase refactor
(primitive extraction, marketing-number truth-up plus docs,
JS→TS migration with Zod validation, gallery virtualization) and
the July 2026 site redesign; set up the Vercel + GitHub Actions +
Vitest + Husky pipeline. I use the service across every personal
repo as a live test signal for the project itself.
### github-readme-suno-cards — Suno Music Cards for GitHub READMEs — GitHub Marketplace Action + Vercel-hosted SVG service that turns any Suno creator's catalog into auto-updating animated cards in their GitHub README — three id input formats (UUID / suno.com URL / short code), 36 GitHub Action inputs, 2 layouts x 2 presets x 3 themes (12 base combinations), CJK-safe text via SVG foreignObject + CSS keyframe equalizer. Public-extracted from a private monorepo on 2026-04-13 and now shares the @suno-cards/parser kernel with Seismophone.
[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-07-17]
- **URL:** https://github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/github-readme-suno-cards
- **Tech stack:** pnpm 9.12 monorepo (apps/ + packages/ + action/), Next.js 15.1 (Vercel serverless runtime), TypeScript 5.6, @suno-cards/parser workspace package (Valibot-validated Suno oEmbed parser — shared kernel with Seismophone), @suno-cards/render workspace package (pure-function SVG renderer), Valibot (strict TypeScript schema validation against studio-api-prod.suno.com oEmbed), SVG <foreignObject> + inline HTML <span> (CJK text shaping), CSS @keyframes (equalizer-bar animation driven by HTML spans inside the SVG), GitHub Actions composite action (Node 20 runner with esbuild-bundled dist), Biome 1.9 (lint + format), Vitest (parser tests inside action/test/), Vercel Data Cache (upstream, 3600s TTL for single cards), HTTP Cache-Control headers (downstream, drives GitHub Camo proxy), esbuild (action bundle build via action/build.ts), MIT license
- **Related projects:** Seismophone — The independent observatory for AI music

**Summary:** Show your AI-generated Suno music as animated, always-current
cards on your GitHub profile, portfolio, or docs. Paste one
line for a single song, or add a scheduled GitHub Action that
keeps a whole catalog in sync — new tracks, play counts, and
likes update on their own. 12 built-in card styles, correct
rendering of Chinese/Japanese/Korean titles, and full
English/Chinese/Japanese localization, from an MIT-licensed
pnpm monorepo running on Next.js 15.1 and Vercel.

**Impact:** Turns any Suno creator's AI-generated songs into animated cards for their GitHub profile — kept up to date automatically. 12 built-in styles, correct display of Chinese/Japanese/Korean titles, and a one-line setup that syncs a whole catalog.

**Problem:** Suno AI creators have no graceful way to surface their music
where developer audiences actually gather: GitHub READMEs,
portfolio sites, technical blog footers, project docs. The
three workarounds all fail. Linking out to suno.com loses the
visual hook and breaks if Suno changes its URL shapes.
Screenshotting the player goes stale the moment you publish a
new song. Hand-editing Markdown is fine for one song and
unmanageable past three. Worse, many Suno creators are
bilingual and release tracks with Chinese, Japanese, or Korean
titles — and plain SVG `<text>` (the usual way to draw text
inside an embeddable image) never inherited HTML's rich
text-shaping rules, so those titles render incorrectly. The
gap: a service that renders animated, visually rich Suno cards
as embeddable images, shapes CJK text correctly, supports both
zero-setup single embeds and CI-driven auto-syncing grids, and
works both as a browse-and-build website and as a fully
programmatic GitHub Action.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. (1) Developer-musicians using
Suno who want their GitHub profile README, portfolio, or
project docs to show a live, auto-updating catalog of their
music — including CJK-named tracks. (2) Music creators more
broadly who keep a GitHub-shaped public presence
(developer-relations folks, indie-game developers, bedroom
producers with side projects, researchers releasing music
under handles) and want a polished visual surface for their
Suno output. (3) Engineers reading the project as a worked
example of (a) extracting a clean public repo from a larger
internal monorepo, (b) sharing a single validated parser
across two independent products, and (c) the
SVG-`<foreignObject>`-plus-CSS-keyframes technique for
animated, CJK-safe cards that plain SVG can't produce.

**Product logic:** Two complementary paths, plus stats surfaces. **Path 1 —
Direct Embed (zero setup).** Paste one Markdown line pointing
at the hosted card endpoint:
`[![](https://github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app/api/card?id=YOUR_SONG_ID)](https://suno.com/song/YOUR_SONG_ID)`.
The `id` accepts three formats — a raw UUID, a full
`suno.com/song/<uuid>` URL, or a `suno.com/s/<shortcode>`
short code — which the parser canonicalises to one internal
ID. The hosted `/builder` lets you preview the 12 base
combinations (2 layouts × 2 presets × 3 themes), flip 11
element toggles, override colors with hex codes, and copy the
finished embed URL. **Path 2 — GitHub Action auto-sync.** Add
`<!-- SUNO-CARDS:START -->` / `<!-- SUNO-CARDS:END -->`
markers to your README and a workflow using
`uses: ChanMeng666/github-readme-suno-cards@v0.1` on a
schedule (typically every 6 hours). The Action reads any of
its 36 inputs — a Suno handle (auto-discovery of every public
song), a YAML manifest of song IDs, or an inline CSV; plus
sorting, tag and duration filters, layout/preset/theme/
language, and color overrides — fetches and ranks the matching
songs, and surgically replaces only the content between the
markers. Beyond single songs, a profile summary card
(`/api/profile`) and an auto-discovered card stack
(`/api/cards`) render whole-catalog views. All surfaces share
one parser and one renderer, so cards look identical whichever
path produced them.

**Technical approach:** A pnpm monorepo with three workspaces: `apps/` (the Next.js
15.1 frontend on Vercel), `packages/` (two reusable kernels),
and `action/` (the GitHub Marketplace Action). The two
packages are the architecturally important part.
**`@suno-cards/parser`** is a Valibot-validated (v0.42.1)
client for Suno's public oEmbed-style API on
`studio-api-prod.suno.com` — anonymous, CORS-enabled,
public-by-design endpoints Suno advertises via the W3C oEmbed
protocol; no scraping, no auth, no headless browsers. The same
parser is shared with Seismophone, a separate Suno analytics
product, so a single schema definition and a single
typed-error hierarchy underwrite both — fix a Suno API drift
once, both benefit. That hierarchy now distinguishes an HTTP
`422` malformed-request (`SunoInvalidRequestError`) from a
`404` unknown-handle (`SunoHandleNotFoundError`), alongside
not-found, private, not-ready, schema, and network error
types. A 2026-07 health audit also quarantined `/api/trending`
— it turned out to be an abandoned September-2024 snapshot,
not a live feed, so `fetchTrending` was removed from the
parser's public exports to stop anyone building on stale data.
**`@suno-cards/render`** is the pure-function SVG engine —
deterministic, cacheable, and callable from both Vercel's
serverless runtime and the Action's Node 20 runner. The
defining rendering technique: SVG `<foreignObject>` carries
inline HTML `<span>`s so CJK text shapes correctly (plain SVG
`<text>` can't), with CSS `@keyframes` animating those spans
into the equalizer bars — a hybrid plain SVG can't produce
cleanly, and one that survives GitHub's Camo image proxy
because CSS keyframes pass its SVG sanitization. Text ships
localized in English, Chinese, and Japanese. Two-level
caching: Vercel's upstream data cache (3600s for single cards)
plus downstream `Cache-Control` headers that let Camo manage
distribution. The Action is built by an esbuild bundle
(`action/build.ts` → committed `action/dist/index.js`, Node
20) so it needs no install step at workflow time. Quality:
Biome for lint/format, Vitest suites across parser, render,
and action.

**Outcomes:** Live production service at
github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app with `/gallery` and
`/builder` routes and three SVG endpoints (`/api/card`,
`/api/profile`, `/api/cards`). The current public repository
is the clean-extract release — first commit `73f4fa8`
"initial commit — github-readme-suno-cards v0.1" on
2026-04-13, immediately followed by `295756e` "clean remaining
internal reference" (the post-extraction sanitization sweep) —
carried forward to its latest commit `86167f4` on 2026-07-06,
which recorded a 2026-07-05 health audit confirming all three
render surfaces against live Suno data. That audit produced
two durable API decisions: the `/api/trending` fossil was
quarantined out of the public parser surface, and HTTP 422 got
its own error type. The shared parser kernel with Seismophone
is the architecturally important outcome: rather than two Suno
integrations drifting apart, both products are pinned to one
Valibot contract and one typed-error hierarchy. Roadmap:
waveform card variant, lyrics-excerpt cards, playlist cards, a
JSON API, and PNG export via `@resvg/resvg`.

**My role:** Sole author across both the original internal monorepo and
the public v0.1 extraction. Designed the pnpm monorepo split
with the parser and renderer as reusable workspace packages;
wrote the Valibot schemas against Suno's oEmbed contract;
designed the typed Suno error hierarchy (including the
422-vs-404 split); engineered the `<foreignObject>` +
CSS-keyframes technique that handles CJK text and animated
equalizers in a single card; built the GitHub Marketplace
Action with its esbuild-bundled dist and its 36-input surface
with three-format ID normalization; designed the
`<!-- SUNO-CARDS:START/END -->` marker-based README
replacement; built the visual `/builder` with live preview
over all 12 base combinations, 11 toggles, and hex overrides;
ran the 2026-04-13 public-extract release and the 2026-07
health audit that quarantined the trending fossil; and
coordinate parser updates across this project and its
Seismophone sibling.

## More Projects (by category)

> The rest of the catalogue: every project not listed above, grouped by category. This is NOT an open-source list — it includes client deliverables and projects with no public repository, and a link here may point to a live site rather than to source code. Treat a project as open source only if it appears under "Notable Open Source Projects" above or carries a repository link on github.com/ChanMeng666.

**web-apps:**
- [She Sharp](https://github.com/NZ-SheSharp/she-sharp) [tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-17] -- live: https://www.shesharp.org.nz/
- [TikTok Shop Go Template](https://github.com/ChanMeng666/douyin-mall-go-template) [tier=archive · recency=deprecated · updated=2026-07-14]
